\[
\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}.
\]

### 2. The support sandwich lemma

We prove the key normal-bidual estimate.

#### Lemma 2.1: support sandwich

For all \(U,W\subset V\), if \(I=U\cap W\), then
\[
q_U q_W q_U\le q_I.
\]

#### Proof

We split the proof into four parts.

---

#### 2.1. Algebraic hereditary cores and support approximate units

For \(U\subset V\), define
\[
\mathcal S_U^{\mathrm{alg}}
=
\{L_aR_b e_U: a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
Let \(\mathcal D_U\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(\mathcal S_U^{\mathrm{alg}}\), equivalently
\[
\mathcal D_U
=
\overline{\operatorname{span}}^{\|\cdot\|}
\{s T t^*: s,t\in \mathcal S_U^{\mathrm{alg}},\ T\in B(L^2M)\}.
\]

For a finite set \(F\subset \mathcal S_U^{\mathrm{alg}}\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), put
\[
A_{F,U}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\qquad
h^U_{F,\varepsilon}
=
A_{F,U}(A_{F,U}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
Then \(h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\in C^*(M,JMJ,e_U)\), \(0\le h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\le 1\), and the net over finite \(F\) and \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\) is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(\mathcal D_U\).

The algebraic core is large enough in the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-topology. Indeed, if \(x,y\in M\) are bounded and \(a,b\in\mathcal A\) are bounded approximants, then
\[
\|L_xR_y e_U-L_aR_b e_U\|_{\infty,2}
\le
\|x-a\|_2\|y\|_\infty
+
\|a\|_\infty\|y-b\|_2.
\]
The same estimate holds for adjoints. Hence finite sums
\[
(L_xR_y e_U)T(L_{x'}R_{y'}e_U)^*
\]
with arbitrary \(x,y,x',y'\in M\) are approximated in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\) by corresponding sums with algebraic coefficients. Thus \(\mathcal D_U\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(\mathcal I_{X_U}\).

Also, because \(e_U=L_1R_1e_U\in\mathcal S_U^{\mathrm{alg}}\), once \(e_U\in F\) one has \(A_{F,U}\ge e_U\), and hence
\[
\|(1-h^U_{F,\varepsilon})e_U\|
\le
\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{1+\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2}.
\]
So these approximate units absorb the large corner \(e_U B(L^2M)e_U\).

By DKEP support convergence,
\[
\iota(h^U_{F,\varepsilon})\xrightarrow{\sigma\text{-weak}} q_U.
\]

---

#### 2.2. Finite propagation through intersections

We need a graph-product Wick-calculus lemma.

Let \(U,W\subset V\) and \(I=U\cap W\). For all \(a,b\in\mathcal A\),
\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
\in
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
L_\alpha R_\beta\, e_J\, L_\gamma R_\delta\, e_W:
\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta\in\mathcal A,\ J\subset I
\right\}.
\tag{2.1}
\]
Similarly,
\[
e_W L_aR_b e_U
\in
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
e_W L_\alpha R_\beta\, e_J\, L_\gamma R_\delta:
\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta\in\mathcal A,\ J\subset I
\right\}.
\tag{2.2}
\]

Here is the proof of (2.1). By linearity, take \(a,b\) to be reduced Wick words. The relative graph-product normal form says that \(a\) can be decomposed, after admissible shuffles and finite expansion inside vertex algebras, as a finite sum of terms
\[
a_U\, \xi\, a_W,
\]
where \(a_U\in\mathcal A_U\), \(a_W\in\mathcal A_W\), and \(\xi\) is reduced relative to \((U,W)\), meaning no letter of \(\xi\) can be shuffled to the far left into \(U\) and no letter can be shuffled to the far right into \(W\). Similarly \(b\) is a finite sum of terms
\[
b_W\,\eta\, b_U,
\]
where \(b_W\in\mathcal A_W\), \(b_U\in\mathcal A_U\), and \(\eta\) is reduced relative to \((W,U)\).

The relative conditional-expectation formula for graph products is the following. If \(\xi\) is \((U,W)\)-reduced and \(\eta\) is \((W,U)\)-reduced, then for \(y\in\mathcal A_W\),
\[
E_U(\xi\, y\, \eta)
=
\lambda_{\xi,\eta}\, E_J(y)
\tag{2.3}
\]
for some scalar \(\lambda_{\xi,\eta}\), possibly zero, and some subset
\[
J\subset U\cap W.
\]
The set \(J\) consists of precisely those vertices in \(U\cap W\) whose letters can commute past all exterior letters in the relative word. Formula (2.3) is just the reduced-word algorithm: every letter outside \(U\) must be cancelled by a matching exterior letter; if the exterior relative words do not match, the scalar is zero; the only letters of \(y\in M_W\) that can remain after projection to \(M_U\) are the letters lying in \(M_{U\cap W}\) and commuting past the exterior letters.

Now for \(z\in\mathcal A\),
\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W\widehat z
=
\widehat{E_U(a\,E_W(z)\,b)}.
\]
Applying the preceding decompositions and (2.3), each summand has the form
\[
E_U\bigl(a_U\,\xi\,(a_W E_W(z)b_W)\,\eta\, b_U\bigr)
=
\lambda_{\xi,\eta}\,
a_U\,E_J(a_W E_W(z)b_W)\,b_U,
\]
with \(J\subset U\cap W\). Therefore the corresponding operator is
\[
\lambda_{\xi,\eta}\,
L_{a_U}R_{b_U}\, e_J\, L_{a_W}R_{b_W}\, e_W.
\]
Summing finitely many such terms proves (2.1). Taking adjoints and exchanging \(U,W\) gives (2.2).

---

#### 2.3. Algebraic triple products land in \(X_I\)

Let \(\mathcal M_0\) be the algebraic multiplier algebra
\[
\mathcal M_0=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
Let \(\mathcal Y_U^0\) be the algebraic \(*\)-algebra generated by the positive algebraic \(U\)-blocks
\[
L_aR_b e_U R_{b^*}L_{a^*},\qquad a,b\in\mathcal A,
\]
and define \(\mathcal Y_W^0\) analogously.

We first record that \(U\)- and \(W\)-blocks act as multipliers of \(X_I\). Applying (2.1) with \(W=I\) gives
\[
e_U T e_I\in X_I\qquad(T\in\mathcal M_0),
\]
because the intermediate projections are \(e_J\) with \(J\subset I\), and
\[
e_J B(L^2M)e_I\subset e_I B(L^2M)e_I\subset X_I.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(e_I T e_U\in X_I\). Hence
\[
\mathcal Y_U^0 X_I,\ X_I\mathcal Y_U^0,\ 
\mathcal Y_W^0 X_I,\ X_I\mathcal Y_W^0
\subset X_I.
\tag{2.4}
\]

Next, let \(A,B\in\mathcal M_0\) and \(T\in B(L^2M)\). By (2.1) and (2.2),
\[
e_U A e_W
=
\sum_i A_i e_{J_i} B_i e_W,
\qquad
e_W B e_U
=
\sum_j e_W C_j e_{K_j}D_j,
\]
with \(J_i,K_j\subset I\). Therefore
\[
e_U A e_W\,T\,e_W B e_U
=
\sum_{i,j}
A_i e_{J_i}
\bigl(B_i e_W T e_W C_j\bigr)
e_{K_j}D_j.
\]
Since \(J_i,K_j\subset I\),
\[
e_{J_i}B(L^2M)e_{K_j}\subset e_I B(L^2M)e_I\subset X_I,
\]
and the outer algebraic multipliers preserve \(X_I\). Hence
\[
e_U A e_W\,T\,e_W B e_U\in X_I.
\tag{2.5}
\]

Combining (2.4) and (2.5), every algebraic triple product satisfies
\[
\mathcal Y_U^0\,\mathcal Y_W^0\,\mathcal Y_U^0\subset X_I.
\tag{2.6}
\]

Each \(h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\) is a norm limit of polynomials with zero constant term in finite sums of algebraic positive \(U\)-blocks, and similarly for \(h^W_{G,\delta}\). Since \(X_I\) is norm closed, (2.6) gives
\[
h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\,h^W_{G,\delta}\,h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\in X_I.
\tag{2.7}
\]

---

#### 2.4. Passing to support projections

Let
\[
A_\lambda=\iota(h^U_\lambda),\qquad B_\mu=\iota(h^W_\mu),
\]
where \(\lambda=(F,\varepsilon)\) and \(\mu=(G,\delta)\). By the support convergence above,
\[
A_\lambda\xrightarrow{\sigma\text{-weak}} q_U,
\qquad
B_\mu\xrightarrow{\sigma\text{-weak}} q_W.
\]

For each \(\lambda,\mu\), the elements \(h^U_\lambda\) and \(h^W_\mu\) lie in
\[
C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W),
\]
so DKEP multiplicativity gives
\[
\iota(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda)
=
A_\lambda B_\mu A_\lambda.
\]
By (2.7), the operator \(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\) lies in \(X_I\), and it is a positive contraction. Thus
\[
0\le A_\lambda B_\mu A_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{2.8}
\]

Fix \(\lambda\) and let \(\mu\) tend to infinity. Multiplication by a fixed element is normal in the von Neumann algebra \(\mathcal N_{\mathrm{nor}}\), so
\[
0\le A_\lambda q_W A_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{2.9}
\]
Now let \(\lambda\) tend to infinity. Since
\[
0\le A_\lambda\le q_U
\quad\text{and}\quad
A_\lambda\xrightarrow{\sigma\text{-weak}} q_U,
\]
we have \(A_\lambda\to q_U\) \(\sigma\)-strongly: indeed,
\[
0\le q_U-A_\lambda\le q_U,
\]
and for every normal positive functional \(\omega\),
\[
\omega\bigl((q_U-A_\lambda)^2\bigr)
\le
\omega(q_U-A_\lambda)\to0.
\]
Therefore
\[
A_\lambda q_W A_\lambda
\xrightarrow{\sigma\text{-weak}}
q_U q_W q_U.
\]
The order interval \([0,q_I]\) is \(\sigma\)-weakly closed, so (2.9) implies
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\]
This proves Lemma 2.1. ∎

### 3. Finite-cover consequence of the sandwich

We need the following elementary state-theoretic observation.

Let \(\phi\) be a state on a unital \(C^*\)-algebra \(A\), and let \(p\in A\) be a projection with \(\phi(p)=1\). In the GNS representation \((\pi_\phi,H_\phi,\xi_\phi)\),
\[
\|(1-\pi_\phi(p))\xi_\phi\|^2
=
\phi(1-p)=0.
\]
Thus \(\pi_\phi(p)\xi_\phi=\xi_\phi\), and consequently
\[
\phi(a)=\phi(pap)\qquad(a\in A).
\tag{3.1}
\]

Now suppose \(U,W\subset V\) and \(\phi(q_U)=\phi(q_W)=1\). By (3.1), applied with \(p=q_U\) and \(a=q_W\),
\[
1=\phi(q_W)=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U).
\]
By Lemma 2.1,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Hence
\[
1
=
\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)
\le
\phi(q_{U\cap W})
\le
1,
\]
so
\[
\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\tag{3.2}
\]

Iterating (3.2), if \(U_1,\dots,U_n\subset V\) and
\[
\phi(q_{U_i})=1\qquad(1\le i\le n),
\]
then
\[
\phi(q_{U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n})=1.
\tag{3.3}
\]

Apply this to
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V.
\]
Because the graph is loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Hence
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore any state \(\phi\) satisfying
\[
\phi(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1\qquad(v\in V)
\]
also satisfies
\[
\phi(q_\varnothing)=1.
\tag{3.4}
\]

### 4. Fixed-vertex relative proper proximality

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad
B=M_L,\qquad
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Then
\[
M=A\ast_B C
\]
trace-preservingly, and
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\qquad
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes\operatorname{id}_B.
\]

We prove that there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). This is stronger than relative proper proximality to \(X_B\).

#### 4.1. Irreducibility and non-neighbors

Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of non-neighbors of \(v\).

Irreducibility implies \(N(v)\ne\varnothing\). Indeed, if \(N(v)=\varnothing\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V\setminus\{v\}\), and
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
is a nontrivial join decomposition.

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(L\ne\varnothing\) because \(|V|\ge3\). If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition: \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(L\) by definition of \(L\), and \(w\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(L\) by assumption. Hence, in the case \(N(v)=\{w\}\), there exists
\[
z\in L
\]
such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\).

#### 4.2. AFP sector projections

Let
\[
A^\circ=\ker(E_B|_A),
\qquad
C^\circ=\ker(E_B|_C),
\]
and
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),
\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The \(L^2\)-space of the amalgamated free product decomposes as
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\substack{n\ge1\\ \epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\\ \epsilon_i\ne\epsilon_{i+1}}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(P_A\) be the projection onto the summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\), and \(P_C\) the projection onto the summands whose first AFP letter is \(C\). Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]
Set
\[
R_A=e_B+P_A,
\qquad
R_C=e_B+P_C.
\]

We claim that
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]

Let \(\rho(x)=R_x=Jx^*J\). Since \(e_B\in X_B\subset \mathcal I_{X_B}\), and \(\mathcal I_{X_B}\) is a \(JMJ\)-bimodule,
\[
[e_B,\rho(x)]\in\mathcal I_{X_B}\qquad(x\in M).
\tag{4.1}
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\overline D\) denote the other algebra. If \(d\in D^\circ\), right multiplication by \(d\) affects only the last AFP letter. Therefore it preserves \(R_D L^2(M)\) and also its orthogonal complement. Right multiplication by \(B\) preserves every AFP sector. Hence
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0\qquad(x\in D).
\tag{4.2}
\]
Since
\[
R_{\overline D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
(4.1) and (4.2) give
\[
[R_{\overline D},\rho(x)]\in\mathcal I_{X_B}\qquad(x\in D).
\]
Thus for \(T=R_A\) and \(T=R_C\),
\[
[T,\rho(x)]\in\mathcal I_{X_B}\qquad(x\in A\cup C).
\]

Because \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), the DKEP generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in X_B\subset S_{X_B}(M)\),
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
also belong to \(S_{X_B}(M)\).

#### 4.3. Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\), and define
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=0,
\]
so \(a\in A^\circ\).

We now construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) such that
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\tag{4.3}
\]

If \(|N(v)|\ge2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\). Let \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\) be trace-zero unitaries and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Since \(w_i\notin L\), each \(c_i\) is a reduced word containing a vertex outside \(L\), so
\[
E_B(c_i)=0.
\]
Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\), and for \(i\ne j\), the reduced word \(c_i^*c_j\) contains vertices outside \(L\), so
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0.
\]
Thus (4.3) holds.

It remains to consider the case \(N(v)=\{w\}\). By the irreducibility observation above, choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries
\[
u_w\in M_w,\qquad u_z\in M_z\subset B,
\]
and put
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Both are unitaries in \(C\). Since \(w\notin L\),
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_2)=u_z E_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Moreover,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) and \(w\) are not adjacent, the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced. It contains the vertex \(w\notin L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\), and of course \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). Thus (4.3) holds in all cases.

#### 4.4. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_A L^2(M),
\qquad
H_C^+=R_C L^2(M).
\]

If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\): it sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(K_C\), and it prepends a centered \(C\)-letter to every reduced AFP tensor beginning with an \(A\)-letter. Hence
\[
d H_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M).
\tag{4.4}
\]
In particular,
\[
c_i R_A c_i^*\le P_C
\qquad(i=1,2).
\tag{4.5}
\]

Furthermore, since \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), one has \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\). Thus for \(\xi,\eta\in H_A^+\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,(c_1^*c_2)\eta\rangle
=
0,
\]
because \((c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M)\), which is orthogonal to \(H_A^+\). Therefore
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\tag{4.6}
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_A L^2(M)\). Hence
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\tag{4.7}
\]

#### 4.5. No central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\)

Assume, for contradiction, that \(\omega\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). Since \(S_{X_B}(M)\) is an \(M\)-bimodule, centrality implies
\[
\omega(uTu^*)=\omega(T)
\]
for every unitary \(u\in M\) and every \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\).

Apply \(\omega\) to (4.6). We get
\[
2\omega(R_A)\le \omega(P_C).
\tag{4.8}
\]
Apply \(\omega\) to (4.7). We get
\[
\omega(R_C)\le \omega(P_A).
\tag{4.9}
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\omega(e_B),\qquad
\beta=\omega(P_A),\qquad
\gamma=\omega(P_C).
\]
Since \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge0,
\qquad
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also,
\[
\omega(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,
\qquad
\omega(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus (4.8) and (4.9) become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\tag{4.10}
\]
and
\[
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\tag{4.11}
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta
\ge
\alpha+\gamma
\ge
\alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)
=
3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Hence
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Since \(\alpha,\beta\ge0\), this forces
\[
\alpha=\beta=0.
\]
Then (4.11) gives \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. In particular, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary,
\[
M\ \text{is properly proximal relative to }X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\quad\text{for every }v\in V.
\tag{4.12}
\]

### 5. \(M\) has no amenable direct summand

Assume, toward contradiction, that there is a nonzero central projection \(z\in Z(M)\) such that \(zM\) is amenable. Let
\[
H_z=zL^2(M)=L^2(zM).
\]
Amenability of \(zM\) gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(H_z)\) extending the normalized trace
\[
\tau_z(x)=\frac{\tau(x)}{\tau(z)}
\qquad(x\in zM).
\]

Fix \(v\in V\), and put \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). For \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)
=
\psi\bigl(zTz|_{H_z}\bigr).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\psi\bigl((zx)(zTz)\bigr)
=
\psi\bigl((zTz)(zx)\bigr)
=
\Phi(Tx),
\]
because \(zx\in zM\) and \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central. Thus \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central.

This contradicts Section 4, which proves that \(S_{X_B}(M)\) has no \(M\)-central state. Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

### 6. Final upgrade to absolute proper proximality

Assume, for contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. The algebra \(M\) is separable because \(V\) is finite and each \(M_v\) is separable. By Section 5, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. Therefore the DKEP obstruction theorem gives an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual compactification, normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{6.1}
\]

By (4.12), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every \(v\in V\). The DKEP compression criterion therefore gives
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad(v\in V).
\tag{6.2}
\]

Applying the finite-cover consequence (3.4) to the state \(\Omega\), we obtain
\[
\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\tag{6.3}
\]

Finally, we identify \(q_\varnothing\). Since \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), the projection \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). The hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\) hull of \(e_\varnothing\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\): indeed,
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
\]
is the rank-one operator \(\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}\), and \(M\widehat 1\) is dense in \(L^2(M)\). Conversely, the hereditary hull of a rank-one projection is contained in the compact operators. Therefore
\[
X_\varnothing=K(L^2M),
\]
and hence
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{6.4}
\]

Combining (6.3) and (6.4),
\[
\Omega(q_K)=1,
\]
contradicting (6.1). Thus \(M\) is properly proximal. ∎

## Original Problem

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

## Problem Solved

We prove the following self-contained form of the stated theorem. Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite loopless simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\), and assume that \(\Gamma\) is irreducible in the standard nontrivial-join sense: there are no nonempty disjoint subsets \(V_1,V_2\subset V\) with \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) such that every \(v\in V_1\) is adjacent to every \(w\in V_2\). For each \(v\in V\), let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra and assume that \(M_v\) contains a unitary \(u_v\) satisfying \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let \((M,\tau)=\star_\Gamma (M_v,\tau_v)\) be the tracial graph-product von Neumann algebra, namely the von Neumann algebra generated by trace-preserving copies of the \(M_v\)’s, with \(M_v\) and \(M_w\) commuting whenever \((v,w)\in E\), and with trace \(\tau\) killing all nonempty reduced words in centered vertex letters. Then \(M\) is properly proximal in the standard DKEP/Boutonnet–Ioana–Peterson sense.

This is not a relaxation of the original problem: \(\varphi_v\) is denoted \(\tau_v\), and the irreducibility hypothesis is the standard nontrivial-join irreducibility used in the theorem.

## Proof

### 1. Graph-product notation and DKEP boundary notation

For \(v\in V\), write
\[
M_v^\circ=\ker \tau_v.
\]
The algebraic graph product is spanned by words
\[
x_1x_2\cdots x_n,\qquad x_i\in M_{v_i}^\circ,
\]
modulo the relations that letters from adjacent vertices commute. A word is reduced if no two equal vertex letters can be shuffled next to one another using only commutations along edges. The tracial graph product \(M\) is the GNS von Neumann algebra for the trace \(\tau\) satisfying \(\tau(1)=1\) and \(\tau\) vanishes on every nonempty reduced word.

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the parabolic subalgebra generated by \(\{M_v:v\in U\}\), with the convention \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation; on reduced words, \(E_U\) fixes words using only vertices in \(U\) and kills all reduced words containing a vertex outside \(U\). Let
\[
e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)
\]
be the Jones projection.

For \(v\in V\),
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}:(v,w)\in E\},
\qquad
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\{v\}\cup \operatorname{Link}(v).
\]

Let \(H=L^2(M,\tau)\), and let \(J\) be the modular conjugation, so \(J\widehat x=\widehat{x^*}\). We write
\[
L_a\widehat x=\widehat{ax},
\qquad
R_b\widehat x=\widehat{xb},
\qquad
R_b=Jb^*J.
\]

For \(U\subset V\), the DKEP boundary piece associated with \(M_U\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra
\[
X_U
=
\overline{\operatorname{span}}^{\|\cdot\|}
\{sTt^*:s,t\in \mathscr R_U,\ T\in B(H)\},
\]
where
\[
\mathscr R_U=\{L_aR_b e_U:a,b\in M\}.
\]
Equivalently, \(X_U\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra of \(B(H)\) generated by all left-right translates of \(e_U\).

We use the following DKEP facts as external input.

**DKEP Fact 1: relative proper proximality via boundary operator systems.**  
For a boundary piece \(X\), let \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\) be the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(X\), and define
\[
S_X(M)
=
\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,R_x]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \text{for all }x\in M\}.
\]
This is an operator system containing \(M\). One may check the commutator condition on any von Neumann generating set of \(M\). The algebra \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state \(\phi\) on \(S_X(M)\), normal on \(M\), where \(M\)-central means
\[
\phi(xT)=\phi(Tx)\qquad(x\in M,\ T\in S_X(M)).
\]

**DKEP Fact 2: normal-bidual supports and obstruction.**  
All boundary pieces have support projections \(q_X\) in a common ambient normal-bidual \(C^*\)-algebra. For \(X_U\), write \(q_U=q_{X_U}\). The projection \(q_U\) commutes with the normal-bidual images of \(M\) and \(JMJ\). If \(K(H)\) is the ordinary compact ideal, write \(q_K\) for its support.

If \(M\) is separable, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there is an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual compactification of \(M\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

**DKEP Fact 3: support compression.**  
Let \(X\) be a boundary piece with support \(q_X\). If an obstruction state \(\Omega\) as in Fact 2 exists and \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\), then
\[
\Omega(q_X)=1.
\]
Indeed, if \(r=1-q_X\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), then
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)},\qquad T\in S_X(M),
\]
defines an \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\), normal on \(M\), contradicting relative proper proximality.

**DKEP Fact 4: approximate-unit support and restricted multiplicativity.**  
Let \(D\subset X_U\) be a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra that is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_U}^{\infty,1}(M)\). If \((h_\lambda)\) is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D\), then its normal-bidual images converge weak-* to \(q_U\). Also, the normal-bidual embedding is not globally multiplicative, but for fixed \(U,W\subset V\) it is multiplicative on
\[
C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W).
\]

We now prove the theorem.

---

### 2. Link-relative proper proximality

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad B=M_L,\qquad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
The graph-product normal form gives the star-link amalgamated free product decomposition
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]
Moreover,
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B,
\]
because \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(L\).

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP Hilbert-space decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n,\ \epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus the summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly. Set
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

We first show
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(D^\circ=\ker(E_B|_D)\). Right multiplication by \(x\in D^\circ\) changes only the last AFP letter. Hence it preserves the subspace \(R_D L^2(M)\), so
\[
[R_D,R_x]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B
\]
for the other algebra \(\bar D\), we get
\[
[R_{\bar D},R_x]=[e_B,R_x].
\]
For \(b\in B\), right multiplication by \(b\) preserves every AFP sector, so the corresponding commutators vanish. Thus, for every \(x\in A\cup C\),
\[
[R_A,R_x],\ [R_C,R_x]\in K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M),
\]
because each possible nonzero commutator is of the form \(e_BR_y-R_y e_B\), which factors through \(L^2(B)\) and hence belongs to the boundary ideal associated with \(e_B\). Since \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), DKEP Fact 1 gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Also \(e_B\in X_B\subset S_{X_B}(M)\), hence
\[
P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]

Now choose ping-pong unitaries. Since
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
the given trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\) gives
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A^\circ:=\ker(E_B|_A).
\]

We need two \(B\)-orthogonal unitaries in \(C^\circ\). Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of non-neighbors of \(v\). Irreducibility implies \(N(v)\ne\varnothing\); otherwise \(V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})\) would be a nontrivial join.

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\subset C.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C^\circ\), and
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]
Indeed, the diagonal terms are \(1\), while for \(i\ne j\), the product \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) is a reduced word containing vertices outside \(L\), so its \(B\)-expectation vanishes.

Now suppose \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Since \(|V|\ge 3\), the link \(L\) is nonempty. If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join, contradicting irreducibility. Hence choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\) are unitaries. We have
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_1)=E_B(c_2^*c_2)=1,
\]
and
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced; the two \(w\)-letters cannot be shuffled together and canceled. It contains the vertex \(w\notin L\), hence
\[
E_B(u_w^*u_z u_w)=0.
\]
Thus again
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_A L^2(M),\qquad H_C^+=R_C L^2(M).
\]
For any unitary \(d\in C^\circ\), left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\): it sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(K_C\), and it prepends a centered \(C\)-letter to every reduced AFP word beginning with \(A\). Therefore
\[
dR_A d^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying this to \(c_1,c_2\), and using \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), the ranges \(c_1H_A^+\) and \(c_2H_A^+\) are orthogonal. Hence
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]
Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\),
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

Now suppose \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries,
\[
\phi(uTu^*)=\phi(T)
\]
for \(u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}\). Thus
\[
2\phi(R_A)
=
\phi(c_1R_Ac_1^*)+\phi(c_2R_Ac_2^*)
\le \phi(P_C),
\]
and
\[
\phi(R_C)=\phi(aR_Ca^*)\le \phi(P_A).
\]
Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Then
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,\qquad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1,
\]
and
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad \phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
The inequalities become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining them,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Thus \(\alpha=\beta=0\), and then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1\).

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). By DKEP Fact 1, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every \(v\in V\).

---

### 3. No amenable direct summand

Suppose \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) and \(zM\) is amenable. Then there exists a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(L^2(zM))\) extending the normalized trace on \(zM\).

Fix \(v\in V\), and let \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). For \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi\bigl(zTz|_{zL^2(M)}\bigr).
\]
This is a state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\psi\bigl((zx)(zTz)\bigr)
=
\psi\bigl((zTz)(zx)\bigr)
=
\Phi(Tx),
\]
so \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. This contradicts the link-relative ping-pong result above. Therefore \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### 4. The support-sandwich lemma

We next prove the key support relation.

**Lemma.** For all \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\]

#### 4.1. Algebraic core

Choose for each \(v\in V\) a unital \(*\)-subalgebra \(\mathcal A_v\subset M_v\) that is \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense. Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by reduced words in letters from the \(\mathcal A_v\)’s. Then \(\mathcal A\) is \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense in \(M\).

For \(S\subset V\), put
\[
\mathscr S_S=\{L_aR_b e_S:a,b\in \mathcal A\},
\]
and let
\[
D_S
=
\overline{\operatorname{span}}^{\|\cdot\|}
\{sTt^*:s,t\in \mathscr S_S,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Then \(D_S\subset X_S\) is a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra.

The algebraic core \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Indeed, if \(x,y\in M\) and \(a,b\in\mathcal A\) are bounded \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-approximants, then
\[
\|L_xR_y e_S-L_aR_b e_S\|_{\infty,2}
\le
\|y\|_\infty\|x-a\|_2+\|a\|_\infty\|y-b\|_2.
\]
The same estimate holds for adjoints, and the DKEP Cauchy-Schwarz estimate for the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-topology gives density for products \(sTt^*\).

For finite \(F\subset \mathscr S_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), define
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
The net \((h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\), ordered by enlarging \(F\) and decreasing \(\varepsilon\), is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D_S\). By DKEP Fact 4,
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})
\longrightarrow q_S
\]
weak-* in the ambient normal bidual.

#### 4.2. Finite propagation

Let \(U,W\subset V\), and put \(I=U\cap W\). We claim that for algebraic \(a,b\in\mathcal A\),
\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
\in
\operatorname{span}
\{A e_J B e_W:A,B\in \operatorname{span}(MJMJ),\ J\subset I\}.
\tag{4.1}
\]
Here \(A,B\) are finite algebraic left-right multipliers.

It suffices to prove this for reduced Wick words \(a,b\). By the relative graph-product normal form, after admissible shuffling of commuting letters one can write
\[
a=a_Ua_0a_W,\qquad b=b_Wb_0b_U,
\]
where
\[
a_U,b_U\in \mathcal A_U,\qquad a_W,b_W\in \mathcal A_W,
\]
and the middle words \(a_0,b_0\) are reduced relative to the pair \((U,W)\) in the sense that no further \(U\)-letter can be split off on the left and no further \(W\)-letter can be split off on the right.

For \(z\in\mathcal A_W\),
\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W(\widehat z)
=
\widehat{E_U(azb)}.
\]
Since \(E_U\) is \(M_U\)-bimodular,
\[
E_U(azb)
=
a_U E_U\bigl(a_0(a_Wzb_W)b_0\bigr)b_U.
\]
The graph-product relative Wick formula says that
\[
E_U\bigl(a_0 y b_0\bigr)
=
\lambda\, E_J(y),
\qquad y\in M_W,
\]
for some scalar \(\lambda\), and for some \(J\subset U\cap W\) determined by the letters of the relative middle word; if the middle words do not match, then \(\lambda=0\). Intuitively, only those \(W\)-letters lying in \(U\cap W\) and commuting past the middle word can survive the \(U\)-expectation.

Thus
\[
E_U(azb)
=
\lambda\, a_U E_J(a_Wzb_W)b_U,
\]
and hence
\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
=
\lambda\, L_{a_U}R_{b_U} e_J L_{a_W}R_{b_W} e_W
\]
with \(J\subset I\). This proves (4.1), and taking adjoints gives the analogous formula for \(e_W L_aR_b e_U\).

#### 4.3. Triple products land in \(X_I\)

Let \(\mathcal M_0=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\}\). For \(S\subset V\), let \(\mathcal Y_S^0\) be the algebraic \(*\)-algebra spanned by words
\[
A_0 e_S A_1 e_S\cdots e_S A_n,
\qquad n\ge 1,\quad A_i\in\mathcal M_0.
\]
Each positive block
\[
(L_aR_b e_S)(L_aR_b e_S)^*
=
L_aR_b e_S R_b^*L_a^*
\]
belongs to \(\mathcal Y_S^0\). Therefore every approximate-unit element \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) belongs to the norm closure of \(\mathcal Y_S^0\), because it is obtained by continuous functional calculus from finite sums of such positive blocks, using a function vanishing at \(0\).

We now show
\[
\mathcal Y_U^0\,\mathcal Y_W^0\,\mathcal Y_U^0\subset X_I.
\tag{4.2}
\]
First observe that, since \(I\subset U,W\), finite propagation implies that \(\mathcal Y_U^0\) and \(\mathcal Y_W^0\) multiply \(X_I\) into itself on both sides. Indeed, on dense generators of \(X_I\) of the form
\[
A e_I T e_I B,
\qquad A,B\in \mathcal M_0,\ T\in B(H),
\]
one repeatedly applies (4.1) to subwords \(e_U A e_I\), \(e_I A e_U\), \(e_W A e_I\), and \(e_I A e_W\), each time factoring through some \(e_J\) with \(J\subset I\). Since \(e_J\le e_I\), the resulting terms remain in \(X_I\).

Now take elementary words \(Y_U,Y_W,Y_U'\) from \(\mathcal Y_U^0,\mathcal Y_W^0,\mathcal Y_U^0\). In the product \(Y_UY_WY_U'\), there is a \(U\)-to-\(W\) junction \(e_UAe_W\) and a \(W\)-to-\(U\) junction \(e_WBe_U\). By (4.1), both factor through projections \(e_J,e_K\) with \(J,K\subset I\). Thus each resulting term has a middle factor of the form
\[
e_J T e_K
\]
for some \(T\in B(H)\). Since \(J,K\subset I\),
\[
e_JTe_K\in e_I B(H)e_I\subset X_I.
\]
The remaining left and right factors multiply \(X_I\) into itself, as observed above. Hence (4.2) follows.

By norm closure,
\[
h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\in X_I
\tag{4.3}
\]
for all algebraic support approximate-unit elements \(h^U_\lambda,h^W_\mu\).

#### 4.4. Passing to support projections

Let
\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda),
\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^W_\mu).
\]
Then
\[
a_\lambda\to q_U,\qquad b_\mu\to q_W
\]
weak-*.

By DKEP Fact 4, \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is multiplicative on
\[
C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W),
\]
which contains all \(h^U_\lambda,h^W_\mu\). Thus
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda).
\]
By (4.3), the operator \(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\) lies in \(X_I\). It is a positive contraction, so its normal-bidual image is dominated by the support \(q_I\):
\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Letting \(\mu\to\infty\) gives
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Since \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\) and \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) weak-*, we have \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) strongly. Indeed,
\[
0\le q_U-a_\lambda\le q_U,
\]
and for every normal positive functional \(\omega\),
\[
\omega((q_U-a_\lambda)^2)
\le
\omega(q_U-a_\lambda)\to 0.
\]
Therefore
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
weak-*. The order interval \([0,q_I]\) is weak-* closed, hence
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\]
This proves the support-sandwich lemma.

---

### 5. Final obstruction argument

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. We already proved that \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. Therefore DKEP Fact 2 gives an \(M\)-central obstruction state \(\Omega\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

For each \(v\in V\), Section 2 proved that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\[
X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]
By DKEP Fact 3,
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad(v\in V).
\]

We now use the support-sandwich lemma to pass from the links to their intersection. Let \(\phi\) be a state on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra containing all \(q_U\)’s and their products; if necessary, extend \(\Omega\) from the operator system to such a state. If \(\phi(q_U)=\phi(q_W)=1\), then in the GNS representation \((\pi_\phi,H_\phi,\xi_\phi)\),
\[
\pi_\phi(q_U)\xi_\phi=\xi_\phi.
\]
Thus
\[
\phi(q_W)=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U).
\]
Using
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
we get
\[
1=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)\le \phi(q_{U\cap W})\le 1,
\]
so
\[
\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]
Iterating over the finite family \(\{\operatorname{Link}(v):v\in V\}\) yields
\[
\Omega\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Because \(\Gamma\) is loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link, hence
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]

It remains to identify \(q_\varnothing\). Since \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), the projection \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). For \(a,b\in M\),
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
\]
is the rank-one operator
\[
\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}.
\]
Because \(M\widehat 1\) is dense in \(L^2(M)\), the boundary piece \(X_\varnothing\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\). Hence
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]
Thus
\[
1=\Omega(q_\varnothing)=\Omega(q_K)=0,
\]
a contradiction.

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal. ∎

**Claim (link-relative form).**  
Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\), and assume \(\Gamma\) is not a nontrivial join. Let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras, and assume every \(M_v\) contains a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\star_{\Gamma}(M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph-product von Neumann algebra. Then, for every \(v\in V\), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to the DKEP boundary piece \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) associated with \(M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Equivalently, there is no \(M\)-central state on the associated operator system \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.

Consequently, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### 1. Boundary notation and the relative criterion

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with \(M_{\varnothing}=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and let
\[
e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)
\]
be the Jones projection.

Write \(H=L^2(M)\). For \(a,b\in M\), let
\[
L_a\widehat x=\widehat{ax},\qquad \rho_b\widehat x=\widehat{xb}.
\]
Thus \(\rho_b=Jb^*J\).

The DKEP boundary piece associated with \(M_U\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra
\[
X_U
=
\overline{\operatorname{span}}^{\|\cdot\|}
\{sTt^*:s,t\in \mathcal R_U,\ T\in B(H)\},
\]
where
\[
\mathcal R_U=\{L_a\rho_b e_U:a,b\in M\}.
\]
Let \(K_{X_U}^{\infty,1}(M)\) be the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed ideal generated by \(X_U\), and define
\[
S_{X_U}(M)
=
\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho_x]\in K_{X_U}^{\infty,1}(M)\ \text{for every }x\in M\}.
\]
This is an operator system containing the left copy of \(M\). A state \(\phi\) on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) is \(M\)-central if
\[
\phi(L_xT)=\phi(TL_x)
\qquad (x\in M,\ T\in S_{X_U}(M)).
\]
In the DKEP formulation, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\) exactly when there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.

We will use the standard facts that \(X_U\subset K_{X_U}^{\infty,1}(M)\), that \(S_{X_U}(M)\) is stable under left multiplication by \(M\), and that the commutator condition defining \(S_{X_U}(M)\) may be checked on any von Neumann generating set of \(M\).

A useful elementary observation is the following: for every \(x\in M\),
\[
e_U\rho_x,\ \rho_x e_U\in X_U.
\]
Indeed, \(\rho_x e_U=L_1\rho_x e_U\in \mathcal R_U\), and since \(e_U\in \mathcal R_U\),
\[
\rho_x e_U=(\rho_x e_U)\,1\,e_U^*\in X_U.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(e_U\rho_x\in X_U\). Hence
\[
[e_U,\rho_x]\in X_U\subset K_{X_U}^{\infty,1}(M).
\]

---

### 2. The star-link AFP decomposition

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad B=M_L,
\]
\[
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}=M_{\{v\}\cup L},
\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B,
\]
with \(E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B\).

The standard graph-product normal form gives the amalgamated free product decomposition
\[
M=A*_B C
\]
with respect to the trace-preserving conditional expectations onto \(B\). Thus
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\substack{n\ge 1\\ \epsilon_1,\dots,\epsilon_n\in\{A,C\}\\ \epsilon_i\ne \epsilon_{i+1}}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]

Let \(P_A\) be the projection onto the sum of all reduced AFP summands whose first letter is \(A\), and define \(P_C\) analogously. Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]
Set
\[
F_A=e_B+P_A,\qquad F_C=e_B+P_C.
\]

We claim that
\[
F_A,F_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\bar D\) denote the other algebra. Right multiplication by an element of \(D\) preserves the subspace \(F_DH\) and its orthogonal complement. Indeed, a centered \(D\)-letter changes only the last AFP tensor; it cannot change whether the first tensor is \(D\), except that it sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(K_D\), which is still contained in \(F_DH\). Elements of \(B\) preserve every AFP sector. Therefore
\[
[F_D,\rho_x]=0\qquad (x\in D).
\]
Since
\[
F_D=1-F_{\bar D}+e_B,
\]
for \(x\in \bar D\) we get
\[
[F_D,\rho_x]=[e_B,\rho_x]\in X_L.
\]
Because \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), the defining commutator condition holds for all \(x\in M\), so
\[
F_A,F_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]
Also \(e_B\in S_{X_L}(M)\), hence
\[
P_A=F_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=F_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_L}(M)\).

---

### 3. Ping-pong unitaries

Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of vertices not adjacent to \(v\).

Because \(\Gamma\) is not a nontrivial join, \(N(v)\neq\varnothing\). Indeed, if \(N(v)=\varnothing\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(V\setminus\{v\}\), so
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
is a nontrivial join decomposition.

We first choose a unitary in \(A^\circ:=\ker(E_B|_A)\). Since
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B
\]
and \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\), the unitary
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A
\]
satisfies
\[
E_B(a)=0.
\]
Thus \(a\in A^\circ\).

Next we choose two \(B\)-orthogonal unitaries in \(C^\circ:=\ker(E_B|_C)\).

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\subset C,\qquad i=1,2.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C^\circ\), and
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]
For \(i=j\) this is clear. For \(i\neq j\), \(c_i^*c_j=u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) is a reduced graph-product word involving vertices outside \(L\), so its \(B\)-expectation is zero.

Now suppose \(|N(v)|=1\), say \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Since \(|V|\ge 3\), the link \(L\) is nonempty. If \(w\) were adjacent to every vertex in \(L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition, because \(v\) is adjacent to all vertices of \(L\) by definition of \(L\), and \(w\) would be adjacent to all vertices of \(L\) by assumption. Therefore there exists \(z\in L\) such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\). Define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Both are unitaries in \(C\). Also \(E_B(c_1)=E_B(c_2)=0\), so \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\). Moreover,
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_1)=E_B(c_2^*c_2)=1,
\]
and
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced; the two \(w\)-letters cannot be shuffled together and canceled. Since this reduced word contains the vertex \(w\notin L\), its \(B\)-expectation is zero. Hence again
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Thus in all cases we have unitaries
\[
a\in A^\circ,\qquad c_1,c_2\in C^\circ
\]
such that
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

---

### 4. The AFP ping-pong inclusions

Left multiplication by an element of \(C^\circ\) sends \(F_AH\) into \(P_CH\). Indeed, a centered \(C\)-letter sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(K_C\), and it prepends a \(C\)-letter to every reduced AFP tensor whose first letter is \(A\). Therefore
\[
L_dF_AH\subset P_CH
\qquad(d\in C^\circ).
\]
In particular,
\[
L_{c_i}F_A L_{c_i}^*\le P_C,
\qquad i=1,2.
\]

Similarly, left multiplication by an element of \(A^\circ\) sends \(F_CH\) into \(P_AH\), so
\[
L_aF_C L_a^*\le P_A.
\]

We also need orthogonality of the two translates \(L_{c_1}F_AH\) and \(L_{c_2}F_AH\). Let \(\xi,\eta\in F_AH\). Then
\[
\langle L_{c_1}\xi,L_{c_2}\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,L_{c_1^*c_2}\eta\rangle.
\]
Since \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), we have \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\). Hence
\[
L_{c_1^*c_2}\eta\in P_CH,
\]
while \(\xi\in F_AH=e_BH\oplus P_AH\), which is orthogonal to \(P_CH\). Therefore
\[
\langle L_{c_1}\xi,L_{c_2}\eta\rangle=0.
\]
Thus the projections \(L_{c_1}F_A L_{c_1}^*\) and \(L_{c_2}F_A L_{c_2}^*\) are orthogonal, and
\[
L_{c_1}F_A L_{c_1}^*
+
L_{c_2}F_A L_{c_2}^*
\le P_C.
\]

---

### 5. No central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Suppose, toward contradiction, that \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(S_{X_L}(M)\) is stable under left multiplication by \(M\), all operators appearing below belong to \(S_{X_L}(M)\).

For every unitary \(u\in M\) and every \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\),
\[
\phi(L_uTL_u^*)=\phi(T),
\]
because
\[
\phi(L_uTL_u^*)
=
\phi((L_uT)L_u^*)
=
\phi(TL_u^*L_u)
=
\phi(T).
\]

Applying this to \(c_1,c_2\) and \(F_A\), we obtain
\[
2\phi(F_A)
=
\phi(L_{c_1}F_A L_{c_1}^*)
+
\phi(L_{c_2}F_A L_{c_2}^*)
\le
\phi(P_C).
\]
Applying it to \(a\) and \(F_C\), we get
\[
\phi(F_C)
=
\phi(L_aF_C L_a^*)
\le
\phi(P_A).
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad
\beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad
\gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Since \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are pairwise orthogonal projections with sum \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,\qquad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Moreover,
\[
\phi(F_A)=\alpha+\beta,
\qquad
\phi(F_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
The two inequalities above become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining them gives
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma
\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)
=
3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Hence
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Since \(\alpha,\beta\ge 0\), we get \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then the inequality
\[
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta
\]
forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\) was arbitrary, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every \(v\in V\).

---

### 6. No amenable direct summand

Suppose \(M\) had a nonzero amenable direct summand. Then there exists a nonzero central projection \(z\in Z(M)\) such that \(zM\) is amenable.

Let \(p=L_z\) be the projection of \(L^2(M)\) onto
\[
pL^2(M)=L^2(zM).
\]
Since \(zM\) is finite amenable, there exists a \(zM\)-central hypertrace, namely a state
\[
\psi:B(L^2(zM))\to \mathbb C
\]
such that
\[
\psi(xT)=\psi(Tx)
\qquad (x\in zM,\ T\in B(L^2(zM))),
\]
and whose restriction to \(zM\) is the normalized trace
\[
\tau_z(zx)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)}.
\]

Fix any \(v\in V\), and again put \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). Define a state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\) by
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(pTp|_{pL^2(M)}).
\]
For \(x\in M\) and \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\),
\[
pL_xTp=(pL_xp)(pTp),
\]
and
\[
pTL_xp=(pTp)(pL_xp),
\]
because \(p=L_z\) commutes with \(L_x\). Also
\[
pL_xp=L_{zx}|_{L^2(zM)}\in zM.
\]
Therefore, by \(zM\)-centrality of \(\psi\),
\[
\Phi(L_xT)
=
\psi((pL_xp)(pTp))
=
\psi((pTp)(pL_xp))
=
\Phi(TL_x).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\).

Its restriction to \(M\) is normal, since
\[
\Phi(L_x)
=
\psi(pL_xp)
=
\tau_z(zx)
=
\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)}.
\]
This contradicts the link-relative proper proximality proved above. Hence no such central projection \(z\) exists, and \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

## Claim (link-relative form)

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\), irreducible in the sense that \(\Gamma\) is not a non-trivial join. Let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras, and assume that each \(M_v\) contains a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\star_{\Gamma}(M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph-product von Neumann algebra.

For every vertex \(v\in V\), writing \(\Lambda=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), the DKEP operator system \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. Hence \(M\) is properly proximal relative to the DKEP boundary piece \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every \(v\in V\). Moreover, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### 1. DKEP boundary notation

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and let
\[
e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)
\]
be the Jones projection.

Put \(H=L^2(M)\). For \(a,b\in M\), let
\[
L_a\widehat x=\widehat{ax},
\qquad
\rho_b\widehat x=\widehat{xb}.
\]
Thus \(\rho_b=Jb^*J\).

The DKEP boundary piece associated with \(M_U\) is
\[
X_U
=
\overline{\operatorname{span}}^{\|\cdot\|}
\{sTt^*:s,t\in \mathcal R_U,\ T\in B(H)\},
\]
where
\[
\mathcal R_U=\{L_a\rho_b e_U:a,b\in M\}.
\]
Let \(K_U=K_{X_U}^{\infty,1}(M)\) be the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed ideal generated by \(X_U\), and define
\[
S_{X_U}(M)
=
\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho_x]\in K_U\ \text{for every }x\in M\}.
\]
A state \(\phi\) on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) is \(M\)-central if
\[
\phi(L_xT)=\phi(TL_x)
\qquad
(x\in M,\ T\in S_{X_U}(M)).
\]

We use the standard DKEP facts that \(K_U\) is a self-adjoint strong \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule, and that for fixed \(T\in B(H)\), the set
\[
\mathcal N_T
=
\{x\in M:[T,\rho_x],[T,\rho_{x^*}]\in K_U\}
\]
is a von Neumann subalgebra of \(M\). Consequently, to prove \(T\in S_{X_U}(M)\), it suffices to check the commutator condition on von Neumann subalgebras generating \(M\).

Also, \(S_{X_U}(M)\) is stable under left multiplication by \(M\) on either side: if \(T\in S_{X_U}(M)\) and \(y\in M\), then
\[
[L_yT,\rho_x]=L_y[T,\rho_x]\in K_U,
\]
and
\[
[TL_y,\rho_x]=[T,\rho_x]L_y\in K_U.
\]

Finally, for every \(x\in M\),
\[
\rho_xe_U,\ e_U\rho_x\in X_U.
\]
Indeed, \(\rho_xe_U=L_1\rho_xe_U\in \mathcal R_U\), and since \(e_U\in \mathcal R_U\),
\[
\rho_xe_U=(\rho_xe_U)1e_U^*\in X_U.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(e_U\rho_x\in X_U\). Hence
\[
[e_U,\rho_x]\in X_U\subset K_U,
\]
so \(e_U\in S_{X_U}(M)\).

---

### 2. The star-link AFP decomposition

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
\Lambda=\operatorname{Link}(v),
\qquad
B=M_\Lambda,
\]
\[
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}=M_{\{v\}\cup\Lambda},
\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(\Lambda\), the graph-product relations give
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B,
\]
and
\[
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]

By the standard graph-product normal form, equivalently the Caspers-Fima star-link decomposition,
\[
M=A*_B C
\]
with respect to the trace-preserving conditional expectations onto \(B\).

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),
\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP Hilbert-space decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\substack{n\ge 1\\ \epsilon_1,\dots,\epsilon_n\in\{A,C\}\\ \epsilon_i\ne \epsilon_{i+1}}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(P_A\) be the projection onto the direct sum of reduced AFP sectors whose first letter is \(A\), and define \(P_C\) similarly. Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C,
\]
where \(e_B=e_\Lambda\). Set
\[
F_A=e_B+P_A,
\qquad
F_C=e_B+P_C.
\]

We claim that
\[
F_A,F_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\bar D\) denote the other algebra. Write
\[
D^\circ=\ker(E_B|_D).
\]
Take \(d\in D\), and decompose \(d=E_B(d)+d^\circ\) with \(d^\circ\in D^\circ\). Right multiplication by \(E_B(d)\in B\) preserves every AFP sector.

On the dense algebraic reduced tensor space, right multiplication by \(d^\circ\) behaves as follows. If a reduced tensor ends in the algebra \(\bar D\), right multiplication by \(d^\circ\) appends a new \(D\)-letter. If it already ends in \(D\), the product of the last \(D\)-letter with \(d^\circ\) decomposes into its \(B\)-expectation plus its centered \(D^\circ\)-part; the \(B\)-part collapses the last tensor, while the centered part remains in a \(D\)-sector. Thus right multiplication by \(d^\circ\) never changes the first letter, except that a one-letter \(D\)-sector may collapse into \(L^2(B)\). Also, \(L^2(B)\) is sent into \(K_D\).

Therefore both \(F_DH\) and its orthogonal complement \(P_{\bar D}H\) are invariant under \(\rho(D)\). Hence
\[
[F_D,\rho_x]=0
\qquad
(x\in D).
\]
For \(x\in \bar D\), using
\[
F_D=1-F_{\bar D}+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[F_D,\rho_x]=[e_B,\rho_x]\in X_\Lambda\subset K_\Lambda,
\]
because \([F_{\bar D},\rho_x]=0\).

Thus the commutator condition holds on both generating subalgebras \(A\) and \(C\). Since \(M=W^*(A,C)\), the DKEP generating-set criterion gives
\[
F_D\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\), it follows that
\[
P_D=F_D-e_B\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M).
\]
Hence
\[
F_A,F_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M).
\]

---

### 3. Choosing the ping-pong unitaries

Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of vertices not adjacent to \(v\).

Irreducibility implies \(N(v)\neq\varnothing\). Indeed, if \(N(v)=\varnothing\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(V\setminus\{v\}\), so
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a non-trivial join decomposition.

First choose a unitary in \(A^\circ=\ker(E_B|_A)\). Under the identification
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
the unitary
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B
\]
satisfies
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1_B=0.
\]
Thus
\[
a\in A^\circ.
\]

We next choose two \(B\)-orthogonal unitaries in \(C^\circ=\ker(E_B|_C)\).

Use the standard graph-product expectation fact: \(E_B\) kills every reduced graph-product word containing at least one vertex outside \(\Lambda\).

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\subset C,
\qquad i=1,2.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C^\circ\), and
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Now suppose \(|N(v)|=1\), say \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Since \(|V|\ge 3\), the link \(\Lambda\) is nonempty. If \(w\) were adjacent to every vertex in \(\Lambda\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \Lambda
\]
would be a non-trivial join decomposition: \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(\Lambda\) by definition of \(\Lambda\), and \(w\) would be adjacent to every vertex of \(\Lambda\) by assumption. Hence there exists \(z\in\Lambda\) such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\).

Define
\[
c_1=u_w,
\qquad
c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Both are unitaries in \(C\). Also,
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_2)=E_B(u_z u_w)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0,
\]
so \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\). Moreover,
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1
\qquad
(i=1,2),
\]
and
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced and contains the vertex \(w\notin\Lambda\). Therefore
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\). Hence again
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Thus in all cases we have unitaries
\[
a\in A^\circ,
\qquad
c_1,c_2\in C^\circ,
\]
such that
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

---

### 4. AFP ping-pong estimates

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\). The reduced-tensor calculation also gives the following left-multiplication inclusion:
\[
L_{d}F_{\bar D}H\subset P_DH
\qquad
(d\in D^\circ).
\]
Indeed, if \(d\in D^\circ\), then \(dL^2(B)\subset K_D\), and if a reduced sector has first letter \(\bar D\), left multiplication by \(d\) prepends a \(D\)-letter, producing a reduced sector with first letter \(D\).

Applying this with \(D=C\) gives
\[
L_{c_i}F_AH\subset P_CH,
\qquad i=1,2.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
L_{c_i}F_A L_{c_i}^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying it with \(D=A\) gives
\[
L_aF_CH\subset P_AH,
\]
hence
\[
L_aF_C L_a^*\le P_A.
\]

We also need orthogonality of the two \(C\)-translates of \(F_AH\). For \(\xi,\eta\in F_AH\),
\[
\langle L_{c_1}\xi,L_{c_2}\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,L_{c_1^*c_2}\eta\rangle.
\]
Since
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0,
\]
we have \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\). Therefore
\[
L_{c_1^*c_2}\eta\in P_CH.
\]
But
\[
F_AH=L^2(B)\oplus P_AH
\]
is orthogonal to \(P_CH\). Hence
\[
\langle L_{c_1}\xi,L_{c_2}\eta\rangle=0.
\]
Thus the projections
\[
L_{c_1}F_A L_{c_1}^*
\quad\text{and}\quad
L_{c_2}F_A L_{c_2}^*
\]
are orthogonal, and
\[
L_{c_1}F_A L_{c_1}^*
+
L_{c_2}F_A L_{c_2}^*
\le P_C.
\]

---

### 5. Absence of \(M\)-central states on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\)

Suppose \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\).

Because \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\) is stable under left multiplication by \(M\), for every unitary \(u\in M\) and every \(T\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\),
\[
\phi(L_uTL_u^*)=\phi(T).
\]
Indeed, \(L_uT\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\), and by \(M\)-centrality,
\[
\phi(T)
=
\phi(L_{u^*}L_uT)
=
\phi(L_uTL_{u^*})
=
\phi(L_uTL_u^*).
\]

Apply this to \(u=c_1,c_2\) and \(T=F_A\). Since the two translates are orthogonal and dominated by \(P_C\),
\[
2\phi(F_A)
=
\phi(L_{c_1}F_A L_{c_1}^*)
+
\phi(L_{c_2}F_A L_{c_2}^*)
\le
\phi(P_C).
\]
Apply the same argument to \(u=a\) and \(T=F_C\). Since
\[
L_aF_C L_a^*\le P_A,
\]
we get
\[
\phi(F_C)\le \phi(P_A).
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),
\qquad
\beta=\phi(P_A),
\qquad
\gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Because \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are pairwise orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,
\qquad
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also,
\[
\phi(F_A)=\alpha+\beta,
\qquad
\phi(F_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
The inequalities above become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining them,
\[
\beta
\ge
\alpha+\gamma
\ge
\alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)
=
3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Thus
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Since \(\alpha,\beta\ge 0\), we get
\[
\alpha=\beta=0.
\]
Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le \beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\). Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary, this holds for every \(\Lambda=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). In particular, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every vertex \(v\).

---

### 6. No amenable direct summand

Suppose, toward contradiction, that \(M\) has a nonzero amenable direct summand. Then there is a nonzero central projection \(z\in Z(M)\) such that \(zM\) is amenable.

Let
\[
p=L_z
\]
be the projection from \(L^2(M)\) onto \(L^2(zM)\). Equip \(zM\) with the normalized trace
\[
\tau_z(zx)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)}.
\]

Since \(zM\) is finite amenable, equivalently injective, there exists a conditional expectation
\[
\Theta:B(L^2(zM,\tau_z))\to zM.
\]
Define
\[
\psi=\tau_z\circ\Theta.
\]
By Tomiyama bimodularity,
\[
\Theta(yT)=y\Theta(T),
\qquad
\Theta(Ty)=\Theta(T)y
\]
for \(y\in zM\). Since \(\tau_z\) is tracial,
\[
\psi(yT)=\psi(Ty)
\qquad
(y\in zM,\ T\in B(L^2(zM))).
\]
Thus \(\psi\) is a \(zM\)-central hypertrace, and
\[
\psi|_{zM}=\tau_z.
\]

Fix any vertex \(v\in V\), and put \(\Lambda=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). Define a state \(\Phi\) on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\) by
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(pTp|_{pL^2(M)}).
\]
Since \(z\) is central, \(p=L_z\) commutes with every \(L_x\), \(x\in M\). For \(x\in M\) and \(T\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\),
\[
pL_xTp=(pL_xp)(pTp),
\]
and
\[
pTL_xp=(pTp)(pL_xp).
\]
Moreover,
\[
pL_xp=L_{zx}|_{pL^2(M)},
\]
which is left multiplication by an element of \(zM\). Therefore, using the \(zM\)-centrality of \(\psi\),
\[
\Phi(L_xT)
=
\psi((pL_xp)(pTp))
=
\psi((pTp)(pL_xp))
=
\Phi(TL_x).
\]
Hence \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\).

Its restriction to the left copy of \(M\) is
\[
\Phi(L_x)
=
\psi(pL_xp)
=
\psi(L_{zx})
=
\tau_z(zx)
=
\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)}.
\]
This is a normal state on \(M\). This contradicts the absence of \(M\)-central states on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\). Therefore \(M\) has no nonzero amenable direct summand.

## Original Problem

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

## Problem Solved

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite loopless simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). For \(v\in V\), write
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}:(v,w)\in E\},
\qquad
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}.
\]
Assume \(\Gamma\) is irreducible in the standard nontrivial-join sense: there is no partition \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) into two nonempty subsets such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). Let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be separable finite von Neumann algebras with faithful normal tracial states, and assume that each \(M_v\) contains a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\star_{\Gamma}(M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra: it is generated by the \(M_v\)’s, adjacent vertex algebras commute, and \(\tau\) kills all nonempty reduced products of centered letters.

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the parabolic graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and let \(e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)\) be the Jones projection. Let \(X_U\subset B(L^2M)\) be the DKEP boundary piece associated with \(M_U\), i.e. the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by operators \(L_x\rho_y e_U\), where \(x,y\in M\), \(L_x\) is left multiplication, and \(\rho_y=Jy^*J\) is right multiplication by \(y\). For a boundary piece \(X\), let
\[
S_X(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\}.
\]
The von Neumann algebra \(M\) is properly proximal in the DKEP sense if there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S(M):=S_{K(L^2M)}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.

The proof below establishes exactly this statement, under the standard nontrivial-join interpretation of irreducibility. Thus it is not a relaxation of the intended original theorem.

---

# Proof

We use the standard graph-product normal form, the star-link amalgamated free product decomposition, the relative graph-product conditional expectation formula, and the DKEP/DE normal-bidual formalism. These are stated explicitly before use.

## Standard external facts used

1. **Graph products and parabolic expectations.**  
   The graph product \(M=\star_\Gamma(M_v,\tau_v)\) contains the \(M_v\)’s, adjacent vertex algebras commute, and the linear span of \(1\) and reduced graph-product words is weakly dense in \(M\). For every \(U\subset V\), the subalgebra \(M_U\) is naturally embedded in \(M\), and there is a trace-preserving conditional expectation \(E_U:M\to M_U\) killing reduced words containing at least one vertex outside \(U\). Caspers–Fima also prove that for every \(v\),
   \[
   M\cong M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}*_{M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
   \]
   ([webusers.imj-prg.fr](https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.fima/Documents/GraphProducts.pdf))

2. **Relative graph-product expectation formula.**  
   If \(V_1,V_2\subset V\), and \(x,\widetilde x\) are graph-reduced relative to \((V_1,V_2)\), then for \(y\in M_{V_1}\),
   \[
   E_{V_2}(\widetilde x^{\,*}yx)
   =
   \tau(\widetilde x^{\,*}x)E_U(y),
   \]
   where \(U\subset V_1\cap V_2\) is the set of vertices adjacent to all letters in the middle relative word; if the relative words are not equivalent, the scalar is \(0\). This is Lemma 3.17 of the graph-product structure paper. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

3. **DKEP boundary pieces and \(S_X(M)\).**  
   DKEP define \(M\)-boundary pieces as hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebras \(X\subset B(L^2M)\) whose multiplier algebra contains weakly dense copies of \(M\) and \(JMJ\). For a subalgebra \(B\subset M\), the boundary piece \(X_B\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(xJyJe_B\). They define \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\), prove that it agrees with the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(K_X(M)\), and prove it is a self-adjoint strong \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

4. **Proper proximality in the operator-system sense.**  
   DKEP define
   \[
   S_X(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\},
   \]
   and \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. For \(X=K(L^2M)\), this is ordinary proper proximality. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

5. **Normal-bidual support formalism.**  
   In the DKEP/DE normal-bidual von Neumann algebra, each compact corner \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\) has a support projection \(q_X\) commuting with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\). The normal-bidual characterization says that relative proper proximality can be tested by absence of central states on the corresponding normal-bidual compactification. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

6. **Approximate-unit convergence and Jones-projection commutation.**  
   DE prove that if \((h_i)\) is an approximate unit for a dense compact-corner subalgebra satisfying the normal-bidual commutation hypotheses, then \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h_i)\to q_X\) weak-*; they also prove that the normal support projection commutes with the image of every Jones projection \(e_N\). ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

7. **DKEP compact-corner obstruction.**  
   We use the following standard consequence of DKEP/DE: if \(M\) is separable tracial, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state
   \[
   \Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
   \]
   on the absolute normal-bidual compactification, normal on \(M\), such that
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0,
   \]
   where \(q_K\) is the support projection of the compact boundary piece \(K(L^2M)\). The condition \(\Omega(q_K)=0\) is precisely the exclusion of the compact-corner/weak-compactness obstruction; if an obstruction state had positive compact-corner mass, DKEP’s weak-compactness/amenability alternative would produce an amenable direct summand.

---

## 1. Graph consequences of irreducibility

Irreducible means that \(\Gamma\) is not a nontrivial join: there is no partition
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\ne\varnothing,
\]
with all cross edges present.

### 1.1. \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\neq V\)

Fix \(v\in V\). If \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V\setminus\{v\}\). Since \(|V|\ge3\), both \(\{v\}\) and \(V\setminus\{v\}\) are nonempty, and
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition. Thus
\[
\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V.
\]

Therefore
\[
N(v):=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
is nonempty for every \(v\).

### 1.2. Unique non-neighbor lemma

Suppose \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \operatorname{Link}(v).
\]
Since \(|V|\ge3\), \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\ne\varnothing\). If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\), then every vertex of \(\{v,w\}\) would be adjacent to every vertex of \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\): this is clear for \(v\) by definition of the link, and true for \(w\) by assumption. Hence
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup\operatorname{Link}(v)
\]
would be a nontrivial join, contradiction. Thus:

> If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), there exists \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\).

---

## 2. Fixed-vertex link-relative proper proximality

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad B=M_L,
\]
\[
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
By the star-link graph-product decomposition,
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]
Also
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\qquad
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]

Let \(H=L^2(M)\), and write \(\rho_x=Jx^*J\) for right multiplication by \(x\).

### 2.1. AFP first-letter sector projections

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP Hilbert space decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where each \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus all reduced AFP sectors whose first letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly. Put
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

We claim
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\bar D\) denote the other algebra. Write
\[
D^\circ=\ker(E_B|_D).
\]
Right multiplication by \(B\) preserves every AFP sector. If \(d\in D^\circ\), right multiplication by \(d\) changes only the last AFP letter. Thus \(R_DH\) and its orthogonal complement are invariant under \(\rho(d)\), so
\[
[R_D,\rho(d)]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(d)]=[e_B,\rho(d)].
\]
But \(e_B\in X_B\), and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers on \(X_B\), so
\[
[e_B,\rho(d)]\in X_B\subset K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M).
\]
For arbitrary \(d\in D\), decompose \(d=E_B(d)+d^\circ\). The \(B\)-part preserves sectors, and the centered part is covered above.

Because \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), the DKEP strong-bimodule generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in S_{X_B}(M)\), also
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_B}(M)\).

### 2.2. Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\). Under
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
define
\[
a=u_v\otimes1_B.
\]
Then
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1_B=0,
\]
so
\[
a\in A^\circ:=\ker(E_B|_A).
\]

We next construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ:=\ker(E_B|_C)\) satisfying
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

#### Case 1: \(|N(v)|\ge2\)

Choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\). Set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Each \(w_i\notin L\), so \(E_B(c_i)=0\). Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). For \(i\ne j\), the word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains vertices outside \(L\), hence its \(B\)-expectation is \(0\). Thus
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

#### Case 2: \(N(v)=\{w\}\)

By the unique non-neighbor lemma, choose
\[
z\in L
\]
such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\subset B\). Define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Moreover,
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_2)=E_B(u_z u_w)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Also
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1.
\]
For the cross-term,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced: the two \(w\)-letters cannot be shuffled together and canceled. This remains true even if \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\). The word contains \(w\notin L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\). Hence again
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

### 2.3. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_AH,\qquad H_C^+=R_CH.
\]

If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) maps \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\). Indeed, \(dL^2(B)\subset K_C\), and if a reduced AFP word begins with an \(A\)-letter, then prepending the centered \(C\)-letter \(d\) gives a reduced word beginning with \(C\). Therefore
\[
dR_Ad^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying this to \(c_1,c_2\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

The two ranges are orthogonal. For \(\xi,\eta\in R_AH\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,c_1^*c_2\eta\rangle.
\]
Since \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\), left multiplication by \(c_1^*c_2\) maps \(R_AH\) into \(P_CH\), which is orthogonal to \(R_AH=L^2(B)\oplus P_AH\). Hence
\[
c_1R_AH\perp c_2R_AH.
\]
Thus
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\tag{2.1}
\]

Similarly, because \(a\in A^\circ\),
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\tag{2.2}
\]

### 2.4. No \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\)

Suppose \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). Since \(S_{X_B}(M)\) is an \(M\)-bimodule, centrality implies
\[
\phi(uTu^*)=\phi(T)
\]
for every unitary \(u\in M\) and \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\).

Applying \(\phi\) to (2.1),
\[
2\phi(R_A)\le \phi(P_C).
\tag{2.3}
\]
Applying \(\phi\) to (2.2),
\[
\phi(R_C)\le \phi(P_A).
\tag{2.4}
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Since \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge0,\qquad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad \phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus (2.3) and (2.4) become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Hence
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
So \(\alpha=\beta=0\), and then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. In particular, there is no \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\), so
\[
M \text{ is properly proximal relative to } X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]
Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to every link boundary piece.

---

## 3. No amenable direct summand and the DKEP obstruction setup

First note that \(M\) is separable: choose countable \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense subsets of each \(M_v\); finite reduced words in those countable sets span a countable \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense subspace.

### 3.1. No amenable direct summand

We do not use a graph-product factor theorem. We prove directly that \(M\) has no nonzero amenable direct summand.

Suppose, toward contradiction, that \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) and \(zM\) is amenable. Let
\[
p=L_z
\]
be the projection of \(L^2(M)\) onto \(L^2(zM)\). Equip \(zM\) with the normalized trace
\[
\tau_z(zx)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)}.
\]
Since \(zM\) is finite amenable, there is a \(zM\)-central hypertrace
\[
\psi:B(L^2(zM))\to\mathbb C
\]
extending \(\tau_z\).

Fix any vertex \(v\), and put \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Define, for \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\),
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(pTp|_{pL^2(M)}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
pL_xp=L_{zx}|_{pL^2(M)}
\]
is left multiplication by an element of \(zM\). Since \(p=L_z\) commutes with \(L_x\), and since \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central,
\[
\Phi(L_xT)
=
\psi((pL_xp)(pTp))
=
\psi((pTp)(pL_xp))
=
\Phi(TL_x).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central.

Moreover,
\[
\Phi(L_x)
=
\psi(pL_xp)
=
\tau_z(zx)
=
\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)},
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal. This contradicts Section 2, which showed that \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state at all.

Therefore \(M\) has no nonzero amenable direct summand.

### 3.2. Normal-bidual notation and obstruction theorem

Let
\[
\mathcal B=(B(L^2M)^\sharp_J)^*
\]
denote the DKEP/DE normal-bidual von Neumann algebra. For a boundary piece \(X\), let
\[
q_X\in\mathcal B
\]
be the support projection, i.e. the identity of the compact corner
\[
(K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\subset \mathcal B.
\]
For \(U\subset V\), write
\[
q_U:=q_{X_U}.
\]
Let \(q_K\) denote the support projection for the compact boundary piece \(K(L^2M)\).

We will use the DKEP compact-corner obstruction in the following form:

> If a separable tracial von Neumann algebra \(N\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(N\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual compactification \(\widetilde S(N)\), normal on \(N\), such that \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

The phrase \(\Omega(q_K)=0\) is where weak compactness is excluded. A central state with nonzero compact-corner mass yields the weak-compactness/amenability obstruction; because we have already ruled out amenable direct summands, the obstruction state may be taken to vanish on \(q_K\).

---

## 4. The support-sandwich lemma

We prove the key support estimate:
\[
\boxed{q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}}
\tag{4.1}
\]
for all \(U,W\subset V\).

Let
\[
I=U\cap W.
\]

### 4.1. Algebraic cores and approximate units

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by \(1\) and reduced Wick words. For \(S\subset V\), let
\[
\mathcal A_S=\mathcal A\cap M_S.
\]
Let
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{L_a\rho_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\qquad
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{L_a\rho_b:a,b\in\mathcal A_S\}.
\]

For \(S\subset V\), define
\[
\mathscr S_S=\{L_a\rho_b e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
Let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra of \(B(L^2M)\) generated by \(\mathscr S_S\).

The algebraic core \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Indeed, by Kaplansky density, if \(x,y\in M\), we can approximate them in \(\|\cdot\|_2\) by uniformly bounded \(a,b\in\mathcal A\). For \(\|m\|_\infty\le1\),
\[
\|(L_x\rho_y e_S-L_a\rho_b e_S)\widehat m\|_2
\le
\|y\|_\infty\|x-a\|_2+\|a\|_\infty\|y-b\|_2.
\]
The same estimate holds for adjoints. Hence hereditary products built from \(L_x\rho_y e_S\) are \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-approximated by algebraic ones. Also \(e_S\in\mathscr S_S\), so the large corner \(e_SB(L^2M)e_S\) is included in \(D_S\).

For finite \(F\subset\mathscr S_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), define
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
The net \((h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\), ordered by inclusion of \(F\) and by \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D_S\). Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) lies in
\[
C^*(M,JMJ,e_S).
\]

Let \(\iota:B(H)\to B(H)^{**}\) be the universal representation and let
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(T)=p_{\mathrm{nor}}\iota(T)p_{\mathrm{nor}}
\]
be the normal-bidual map. This map is not globally multiplicative. However, DE prove that \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with the images of \(M\), \(JMJ\), and every \(e_N\). Hence \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with
\[
\iota\big(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\big).
\]
Therefore \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is a \(*\)-homomorphism on
\[
C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W).
\tag{4.2}
\]

By the DE approximate-unit lemma,
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})
\longrightarrow q_S
\]
weak-* in \(\mathcal B\).

### 4.2. Finite propagation

We need the following finite-propagation statement.

> For all \(S,T\subset V\),
> \[
> e_S\mathcal C e_T
> \subset
> \operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}.
> \tag{4.3}
> \]

It suffices to prove this for \(L_a\rho_b\), where \(a,b\in\mathcal A\) are reduced words. Using the relative graph-product normal form, write
\[
a=a_Sa_0a_T,
\qquad
b=b_Tb_0b_S,
\]
where \(a_S,b_S\in\mathcal A_S\), \(a_T,b_T\in\mathcal A_T\), and the middle words \(a_0,b_0\) are reduced relative to \((S,T)\) in the appropriate left/right sense.

For \(z\in\mathcal A_T\),
\[
e_SL_a\rho_b e_T\widehat z
=
\widehat{E_S(azb)}
=
\widehat{a_S\,E_S(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0)\,b_S}.
\]
Apply the relative expectation formula with \(V_1=T\), \(V_2=S\), \(y=a_Tzb_T\in M_T\), \(\widetilde x^*=a_0\), and \(x=b_0\). If the middle relative words are not equivalent, the expectation is \(0\). If they are equivalent, then
\[
E_S(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0)
=
\tau(a_0b_0)E_J(a_Tzb_T),
\]
where \(J\subset S\cap T\) consists of vertices adjacent to all letters of the middle relative word.

Thus
\[
e_SL_a\rho_b e_T
=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,
L_{a_S}\rho_{b_S}\,e_J\,L_{a_T}\rho_{b_T}.
\]
This belongs to \(\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T\), with \(J\subset S\cap T\). Linearity gives (4.3).

### 4.3. Triple products land in \(X_I\)

Define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}
\{A_0e_SA_1e_S\cdots e_SA_n:
n\ge1,\ A_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) lies in the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\). Indeed,
\[
ss^*\in \mathcal P_S
\]
for \(s\in\mathscr S_S\), hence \(A_{F,S}\in\mathcal P_S\). Since \(\mathcal P_S\) is an algebra and the function \(t\mapsto t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is uniformly approximable on bounded intervals by polynomials with zero constant term,
\[
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\in\overline{\mathcal P_S}^{\|\cdot\|}.
\]

Now define the algebraic \(I\)-corner class
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}
\{A e_J T e_K B:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\),
\[
e_JTe_K\in e_IB(H)e_I\subset X_I.
\]
Because \(M\) and \(JMJ\) multiply \(X_I\),
\[
\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\tag{4.4}
\]

We claim that for \(S=U\) or \(S=W\),
\[
\mathcal P_S\mathcal Z_I\subset\mathcal Z_I,
\qquad
\mathcal Z_I\mathcal P_S\subset\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{4.5}
\]
Indeed, if \(J\subset I\subset S\), then finite propagation gives
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_J
\subset
