E_B(c_i)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\tag{2.1}
\]

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and for \(i\neq j\), the reduced word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) has a vertex outside \(L\), so
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0.
\]

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(L\neq\varnothing\) because \(|V|\ge 3\). There exists \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\); otherwise \(V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L\) would be a nontrivial join. Define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Since \(u_z\in B\) and \(E_B(u_w)=0\),
\[
E_B(c_1)=E_B(c_2)=0.
\]
Moreover,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) and \(w\) are not adjacent, this is a reduced word containing the vertex \(w\notin L\), hence
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Similarly \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\), and of course \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). Thus (2.1) holds in all cases.

Now consider the AFP \(L^2\)-decomposition
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\neq\cdots\neq \epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),
\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus all reduced AFP summands whose first letter is \(A\), and define \(R_C\) similarly. Put
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

We claim that
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\tag{2.2}
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(D^\circ=\ker(E_B|_D)\). If \(x\in D^\circ\), then right multiplication by \(x\) changes only the last AFP letter. Therefore it preserves both the \(R_D\)-sector and its orthogonal complement. Hence
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
For the opposite first-letter projection, using \(R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B\),
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=[e_B,\rho(x)].
\]
Because \(e_B=e_L\in X_L\), and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers of \(X_L\), we have
\[
[e_B,\rho(x)]\in K_{X_L}^{\infty,1}(M).
\]
Elements of \(B\) preserve every AFP sector. Thus, for \(T=R_A\) or \(T=R_C\), the algebra
\[
M_0(T)=\{x\in M:[T,\rho(x)],[T,\rho(x^*)]\in K_{X_L}^{\infty,1}(M)\}
\]
contains both \(A\) and \(C\). By DKEP Lemma 6.1, \(M_0(T)\) is a von Neumann subalgebra; since \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), \(M_0(T)=M\). Hence \(R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(e_B\in X_L\subset S_{X_L}(M)\), also \(P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M)\). This proves (2.2).

Now let
\[
H_A^+=R_AH,\qquad H_C^+=R_CH.
\]
If \(d\in C\) is a unitary with \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\). Indeed, \(dL^2(B)\subset K_C\), and \(d\) sends every reduced word beginning with \(A\) to a reduced word beginning with \(C\). Therefore
\[
dR_Ad^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying this to \(c_1,c_2\), and using \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), we also get orthogonality:
\[
c_1H_A^+\perp c_2H_A^+.
\]
Hence
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\tag{2.3}
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_AH\), so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\tag{2.4}
\]

Suppose \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\) are unitaries in \(M\),
\[
\phi(uTu^*)=\phi(T)
\]
for \(u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}\). Applying \(\phi\) to (2.3) and (2.4) gives
\[
2\phi(R_A)\le \phi(P_C),
\tag{2.5}
\]
and
\[
\phi(R_C)\le \phi(P_A).
\tag{2.6}
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad
\beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad
\gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Then \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0\) and
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Moreover,
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad
\phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus (2.5) and (2.6) become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining them,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Hence \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le \beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1\).

Therefore \(S_{X_L}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. In particular, it admits no \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\). Hence \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_L=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary,
\[
M\text{ is properly proximal relative to }X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\quad\text{for every }v\in V.
\tag{2.7}
\]

### 3. \(M\) has no amenable direct summand

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(0\neq z\in Z(M)\) and \(zM\) is amenable. Let
\[
H_z=zH=L^2(zM),
\]
and normalize the trace on \(zM\) by
\[
\tau_z(x)=\tau(z)^{-1}\tau(x),\qquad x\in zM.
\]
By Connes’s injectivity/amenability characterization, there exists a \(zM\)-central hypertrace
\[
\psi:B(H_z)\to\mathbb C
\]
extending \(\tau_z\).

Fix \(v\in V\), and put \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). For \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{H_z}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\psi((zx)(zTz))
=
\psi((zTz)(zx))
=
\Phi(Tx),
\]
so \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. Also,
\[
\Phi(x)=\tau_z(zx),
\qquad x\in M,
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal. This contradicts (2.7). Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

### 4. Passage from link-relative proper proximality to proper proximality

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, the normal-bidual DKEP criterion gives an \(M\)-central state
\[
\Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
\]
whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.

We first show that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{4.1}
\]
Suppose instead that \(\Omega(q_K)>0\). Define a state on \(B(H)\) by
\[
\Psi(T)
=
\frac{\Omega(q_K\,\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(T)\,q_K)}
{\Omega(q_K)},
\qquad T\in B(H).
\]
Because \(q_K\) commutes with \(M\), \(\Psi\) is \(M\)-central. For \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le q_Kxq_K\le x,
\]
so \(\Psi|_M\) is dominated by a scalar multiple of the normal functional \(\Omega|_M\). Therefore \(\Psi|_M\) is normal. Since \(\Psi\) is \(M\)-central, \(\Psi|_M\) is a normal tracial state, possibly nonfaithful.

Let \(z\in Z(M)\) be the support projection of \(\Psi|_M\). Then \(z\neq 0\), and \(\Psi|_{zM}\) is faithful after normalization. Define, for \(T\in B(zH)\),
\[
\Psi_z(T)
=
\frac{\Psi(zTz)}{\Psi(z)}.
\]
Then \(\Psi_z\) is a \(zM\)-central hypertrace on \(B(zH)\) extending a faithful normal tracial state on \(zM\). Hence \(zM\) is amenable, contradicting Section 3. Thus (4.1) holds.

Now fix \(v\in V\), and write
\[
q_v=q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]
We claim that
\[
\Omega(q_v)=1.
\tag{4.2}
\]
Let \(r=1-q_v\). If \(\Omega(r)>0\), then for \(T\in \widetilde S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)
=
\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
The compression inclusion
\[
q_v^\perp\,\widetilde S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\,q_v^\perp
\subset
q_K^\perp\,\widetilde S(M)
\]
makes this well-defined. Since \(r\) commutes with \(M\), \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. For \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le rxr\le x,
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is dominated by a scalar multiple of the normal functional \(\Omega|_M\), hence is normal. This contradicts the relative proper proximality of \(M\) with respect to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Therefore \(\Omega(r)=0\), which proves (4.2).

Thus
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\text{for every }v\in V.
\tag{4.3}
\]

Let \(\mathcal D\) be the unital \(C^*\)-algebra in the ambient normal bidual generated by \(\widetilde S(M)\) and all projections \(q_U\), \(U\subset V\). Since \(\widetilde S(M)\) is an operator system, the state-extension theorem for operator systems extends \(\Omega\) to a state
\[
\overline\Omega:\mathcal D\to\mathbb C.
\]

If \(\varphi\) is any state on \(\mathcal D\) with
\[
\varphi(q_U)=\varphi(q_W)=1,
\]
then in the GNS representation of \(\varphi\), the cyclic vector is fixed by both projections \(q_U\) and \(q_W\). Therefore
\[
\varphi(q_Uq_Wq_U)=1.
\]
By Lemma 1.1,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
so
\[
\varphi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\tag{4.4}
\]

Applying (4.4) inductively to the family \(\{\operatorname{Link}(v):v\in V\}\), and using (4.3), gives
\[
\overline\Omega\!\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Since \(\Gamma\) is loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Hence
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Thus
\[
\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\tag{4.5}
\]

Finally, \(X_\varnothing=K(H)\). Indeed, \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\), and
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
=
\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b},
\qquad a,b\in M.
\]
Since \(M\widehat 1\) is dense in \(H\), these rank-one operators span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(H)\). Conversely, the boundary piece generated by \(e_\varnothing\) consists of compact operators. Therefore
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{4.6}
\]

Combining (4.1), (4.5), and (4.6),
\[
1=\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=\overline\Omega(q_K)=\Omega(q_K)=0,
\]
a contradiction.

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal. \(\square\)

**Claim.** Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simplicial loopless graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). Assume \(\Gamma\) is **join-irreducible**, i.e. there is no partition
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\ne\varnothing,
\]
such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). Let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras, and assume that each \(M_v\) contains a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Then the tracial graph-product von Neumann algebra
\[
(M,\tau)=\ast_{\Gamma}(M_v,\tau_v)
\]
is properly proximal in the DKEP sense.

We use the standard graph-product interpretation of “irreducible”: no nontrivial join decomposition.

---

## 1. Graph-product preliminaries

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and let \(e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)\) be the Jones projection.

For \(v\in V\), write
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}: w\sim v\},\qquad
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}.
\]

We use the standard reduced-word model for tracial graph products. Reduced graph words span a weakly dense \(*\)-subalgebra \(\mathcal A\subset M\). If a reduced word contains a vertex outside \(U\), then \(E_U\) kills it.

For every \(v\in V\), putting \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), one has a trace-preserving identification
\[
M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ M_L,
\]
under which
\[
E_L|_{M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}}=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_{M_L}.
\]
Moreover,
\[
M
=
M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}
\ast_{M_L}
M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\tag{1}
\]

Since \(V\) is finite and each \(M_v\) is separable, the algebraic reduced-word span coming from countable \(L^2\)-dense subalgebras of the \(M_v\)’s is countable \(L^2\)-dense in \(M\). Hence \(M\) is separable.

---

## 2. DKEP boundary facts used

Let \(H=L^2(M)\), and let
\[
\rho(x)=Jx^*J,\qquad x\in M,
\]
be the right action of \(M\) on \(H\).

For an expected von Neumann subalgebra \(P\subset M\), let \(X_P\) denote the DKEP boundary piece associated with \(P\subset M\). In the present proof we use
\[
X_U:=X_{M_U}.
\]
Concretely, \(X_U\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra of \(B(H)\) generated by the \(M\)- and \(\rho(M)\)-translates of the Jones corner \(e_U B(H)e_U\). Thus \(X_U\neq 0\), \(M\) and \(\rho(M)\) act by multipliers of \(X_U\), and since \(e_\varnothing\le e_U\), we have
\[
K(H)\subset X_U.
\]

Let \(K^{\infty,1}_{X_U}(M)\) be the DKEP \(X_U\)-compact ideal, i.e. the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed \(M\)-\(\rho(M)\)-bimodule generated by \(X_U\). The relative operator system is
\[
S_{X_U}(M)
=
\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho(x)]\in K^{\infty,1}_{X_U}(M)\ \text{for all }x\in M\}.
\]
A state \(\omega\) on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) is \(M\)-central if
\[
\omega(xT)=\omega(Tx)\qquad (x\in M,\ T\in S_{X_U}(M)).
\]

By DKEP Lemma 8.5, for finite separable \(M\), relative proper proximality to \(X_U\) is equivalent to the nonexistence of an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.

We also use the DKEP normal-bidual framework. Let \(\mathcal N\) be the normal-bidual von Neumann algebra, and let
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}:B(H)\to \mathcal N
\]
be the canonical normal-bidual map. For each \(U\subset V\), let \(q_U\in\mathcal N\) be the support projection of \(X_U\). Let \(q_K\) be the support projection of \(K(H)\).

The following DKEP facts will be used.

1. The projections \(q_U\) commute with the left copy of \(M\) and with \(\rho(M)\).

2. If \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, then DKEP Proposition 8.2 gives an \(M\)-central obstruction state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual boundary operator system, normal on \(M\), such that
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0.
   \tag{2}
   \]

3. If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\), then every obstruction state \(\Omega\) as above satisfies
   \[
   \Omega(q_U)=1.
   \tag{3}
   \]
   Indeed, if \(r=1-q_U\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), then the DKEP compression inclusion
   \[
   r\,\widetilde S_{X_U}(M)\,r\subset \widetilde S(M)
   \]
   allows us to define
   \[
   \Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)},\qquad T\in \widetilde S_{X_U}(M).
   \]
   Because \(q_U\), hence \(r\), commutes with \(M\), \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. Moreover, for \(x\in M_+\),
   \[
   0\le r x r\le x,
   \]
   so \(\Phi|_M\) is dominated by a normal functional and is therefore normal. This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X_U\). Hence \(\Omega(q_U)=1\).

---

## 3. Relative proper proximality with respect to vertex links

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad
B=M_L,\qquad
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
By (1),
\[
M=A\ast_B C.
\]

Let
\[
A^\circ=\ker(E_B|_A),\qquad C^\circ=\ker(E_B|_C).
\]
The AFP \(L^2\)-decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\), \(\epsilon_i\ne\epsilon_{i+1}\),
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]

Let \(P_A\) be the projection onto reduced AFP summands whose first letter is \(A\), and let \(P_C\) be the projection onto reduced AFP summands whose first letter is \(C\). Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]
Define
\[
R_A=e_B+P_A,\qquad R_C=e_B+P_C.
\]

### 3.1. The first-letter projections belong to \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Right multiplication by an element of \(A\) preserves \(R_AH\), and right multiplication by an element of \(C\) preserves \(R_CH\). Indeed, right multiplication changes only the terminal AFP letter; it cannot change the initial AFP letter, except that a one-letter word may collapse into \(L^2(B)\), which is still contained in the relevant \(R_DH\).

Hence
\[
[R_A,\rho(a)]=0\qquad (a\in A),
\]
and
\[
[R_C,\rho(c)]=0\qquad (c\in C).
\]

Since
\[
R_A+R_C=1+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[R_C,\rho(a)]=[e_B,\rho(a)]\qquad (a\in A),
\]
and
\[
[R_A,\rho(c)]=[e_B,\rho(c)]\qquad (c\in C).
\]
Because \(e_B=e_L\in X_L\), and \(K^{\infty,1}_{X_L}(M)\) is a closed \(M\)-\(\rho(M)\)-bimodule, it follows that
\[
[R_A,\rho(x)],\ [R_C,\rho(x)]\in K^{\infty,1}_{X_L}(M)
\]
for every \(x\) in the algebraic \(*\)-algebra generated by \(A\cup C\).

Now let \(z\in M\). Choose algebraic \(z_i\to z\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\), with \(\sup_i\|z_i\|_\infty<\infty\). The DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-norm satisfies, by noncommutative Hölder,
\[
\|[T,\rho(z_i-z)]\|_{\infty,1}
\le 2\|T\|\,\|z_i-z\|_2
\tag{4}
\]
for every bounded \(T\in B(H)\). Since \(K^{\infty,1}_{X_L}(M)\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed, the commutator condition passes to all \(z\in M\). Therefore
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in S_{X_L}(M)\), also
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_L}(M)\).

### 3.2. Ping-pong unitaries

Because
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\) and set
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=0.
\tag{5}
\]

We next construct two unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) such that
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\tag{6}
\]

Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of non-neighbors of \(v\). Join-irreducibility implies \(N(v)\ne\varnothing\); otherwise \(v\) would be adjacent to every vertex in \(V\setminus\{v\}\), giving the join decomposition
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\}).
\]

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\subset C.
\]
Each \(c_i\) is centered over \(B\), because \(w_i\notin L\). Also \(c_i^*c_j\) is a reduced graph word containing a vertex outside \(L\) when \(i\ne j\), so \(E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0\). Thus (6) holds.

It remains to treat the case \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Since \(|V|\ge 3\), the link \(L\) is nonempty. If \(w\) were adjacent to every vertex of \(L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition. Hence there exists \(z\in L\) with \(z\not\sim w\). Choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\), and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Both are unitaries in \(C\). The words \(u_w\) and \(u_z u_w\) contain the vertex \(w\notin L\), hence have \(B\)-expectation zero. Moreover,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w
\]
is reduced because \(z\not\sim w\), and it contains \(w\notin L\). Thus \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), and similarly \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\). Therefore (6) holds.

### 3.3. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_AH,\qquad H_C^+=R_CH.
\]

If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\). Indeed, \(dL^2(B)\subset K_C\), and if a reduced AFP word starts with an \(A\)-letter, then prepending a centered \(C\)-letter produces a reduced word starting with \(C\). Hence
\[
dR_A d^*\le P_C.
\tag{7}
\]

Applying this to \(c_1,c_2\), and using \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), the subspaces \(c_1H_A^+\) and \(c_2H_A^+\) are orthogonal. Thus
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*
\le P_C.
\tag{8}
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_AH\). Therefore
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\tag{9}
\]

### 3.4. No central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Suppose \(\omega\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Centrality implies
\[
\omega(uTu^*)=\omega(T)
\]
for every unitary \(u\in M\) and every \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\).

Set
\[
\alpha=\omega(e_B),\qquad
\beta=\omega(P_A),\qquad
\gamma=\omega(P_C).
\]
Since \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,\qquad
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\tag{10}
\]

Applying \(\omega\) to (8) gives
\[
2\omega(R_A)\le \omega(P_C),
\]
hence
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma.
\tag{11}
\]
Applying \(\omega\) to (9) gives
\[
\omega(R_C)\le \omega(P_A),
\]
hence
\[
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\tag{12}
\]
Combining (11) and (12),
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Thus \(\alpha=\beta=0\), and then (12) gives \(\gamma=0\), contradicting (10).

Therefore \(S_{X_L}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. In particular, by DKEP Lemma 8.5, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\[
X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\]
for every \(v\in V\).

---

## 4. \(M\) has no amenable direct summand

Assume toward contradiction that \(z\in Z(M)\) is a nonzero central projection such that \(zM\) is amenable. Since \(zM\) is finite amenable, there exists a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(zL^2(M))\) extending the normalized trace on \(zM\).

Fix \(v\in V\), and put \(X=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). For \(T\in S_X(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{zL^2(M)}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_X(M)\). For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\psi((zx)(zTz))
=
\psi((zTz)(zx))
=
\Phi(Tx),
\]
so \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. This contradicts the conclusion of Section 3, which says that no \(M\)-central state exists on \(S_X(M)\).

Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

## 5. The parabolic support sandwich

We prove that for all \(U,W\subset V\),
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\tag{13}
\]

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra. For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), write
\[
L_aR_b(\widehat{x})=\widehat{axb}.
\]
Let
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
For \(S\subset V\), let
\[
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\cap M_S\}.
\]

### 5.1. Multiplicativity of the normal-bidual map on parabolic algebras

Let \(P\subset M\) be an expected subalgebra, and let \(e_P\) be its Jones projection. In the universal representation of \(B(H)\), let \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) be the normal support projection defining the DKEP normal bidual.

We claim that \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with \(\iota(e_P)\). Indeed, if a representation \(\pi\) of \(B(H)\) is normal on \(M\) and on \(\rho(M)\), then for \(\xi,\eta\) in its Hilbert space and \(x\in M\),
\[
\langle \pi(x)\pi(e_P)\xi,\pi(e_P)\eta\rangle
=
\langle \pi(e_Pxe_P)\xi,\eta\rangle
=
\langle \pi(E_P(x))\pi(e_P)\xi,\eta\rangle.
\]
Since \(E_P\) is normal and \(\pi|_M\) is normal, this vector functional is normal in \(x\). The same argument applies to \(\rho(M)\), using
\[
e_P\rho(x)e_P=\rho(E_P(x))e_P.
\]
Thus \(\pi(e_P)\) preserves the normal part, and since \(e_P\) is self-adjoint, it reduces it. Hence
\[
p_{\mathrm{nor}}\iota(e_P)=\iota(e_P)p_{\mathrm{nor}}.
\]

Applying this to \(P=M_U\) and \(P=M_W\), we get that \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with \(\iota(M)\), \(\iota(\rho(M))\), \(\iota(e_U)\), and \(\iota(e_W)\). Therefore \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is multiplicative on
\[
C^*(M,\rho(M),e_U,e_W).
\tag{14}
\]

### 5.2. Finite propagation for graph-product words

For \(S,T\subset V\), we claim
\[
e_S\,\mathcal C\,e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}
\bigl\{
\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T
\bigr\}.
\tag{15}
\]

It suffices to prove this for \(L_aR_b\), where \(a,b\) are reduced algebraic graph words. Consider the map
\[
x\in\mathcal A\cap M_T
\longmapsto
E_S(axb)\in \mathcal A\cap M_S.
\]

Multiply the three graph words \(a,x,b\) and reduce by the usual graph-product reduction rules: adjacent commuting syllables may be shuffled, and two adjacent syllables from the same vertex algebra are replaced by their scalar part plus centered part. Each reduction branch produces a reduced graph word.

A branch can survive after applying \(E_S\) only if the final reduced word has all vertices in \(S\). Every syllable of \(x\) already has its vertex in \(T\). Hence every syllable of \(x\) that survives in the final reduced word must have vertex in \(S\cap T\). Syllables of \(x\) with vertices in \(T\setminus S\) must be eliminated through cancellations with the finitely many syllables coming from \(a\) and \(b\). Therefore there are only finitely many possible cancellation patterns, depending on \(a\) and \(b\), and after fixing one such pattern, the uneliminated middle part of \(x\) lies in \(M_J\) for some \(J\subset S\cap T\).

For each pattern, the contribution has the form
\[
x\longmapsto
r\,E_J(sxt)\,q,
\]
where \(J\subset S\cap T\), \(r,q\in\mathcal A\cap M_S\), and \(s,t\in\mathcal A\cap M_T\). Thus, as an operator \(L^2(M_T)\to L^2(M_S)\),
\[
e_SL_aR_be_T
\]
belongs to the span of \(\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T\) with \(J\subset S\cap T\). This proves (15).

### 5.3. Algebraic cores for the boundary pieces

For \(S\subset V\), define
\[
\mathscr L_S=\{L_aR_b e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
Let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(\mathscr L_S\), equivalently the norm closure of the span of
\[
sTt^*,
\qquad s,t\in\mathscr L_S,\ T\in B(H).
\]

The algebra \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in the DKEP \(X_S\)-compact ideal. Indeed, if \(x,y\in M\), choose bounded algebraic approximants \(a_i\to x\), \(b_i\to y\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\). Then
\[
\|L_xR_y e_S-L_{a_i}R_{b_i} e_S\|_{\infty,2}
\le
\|x-a_i\|_2\|y\|_\infty
+
\|a_i\|_\infty\|y-b_i\|_2
\to 0.
\]
Using the standard inequality
\[
\|ST\|_{\infty,1}\le \|S\|_{\infty,2}\|T^*\|_{\infty,2},
\]
one obtains \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-density of the algebraic hereditary core.

For a finite set \(F\subset\mathscr L_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), set
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}
=
A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
The net \((h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\) is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D_S\), and by the support-limit property in the DKEP normal bidual,
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\longrightarrow q_S
\]
weak-* in \(\mathcal N\).

### 5.4. Algebraic triple products land in \(X_{U\cap W}\)

Fix \(U,W\subset V\), and put
\[
I=U\cap W.
\]

Let
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}
\{C_0e_SC_1e_S\cdots e_SC_n:
n\ge 1,\ C_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) belongs to the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\), because \(A_{F,S}\in\mathcal P_S\) and the function \(t\mapsto t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is uniformly approximable on bounded intervals by polynomials with zero constant term.

Define
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}
\{A e_J T e_K B:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\) whenever \(J,K\subset I\), we have
\[
\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\tag{16}
\]

By finite propagation (15), if \(S\in\{U,W\}\) and \(J\subset I\subset S\), then multiplying an element of \(\mathcal Z_I\) on the left or right by an element of \(\mathcal P_S\) keeps it inside \(\mathcal Z_I\). Hence
\[
\mathcal P_S\mathcal Z_I\subset \mathcal Z_I,
\qquad
\mathcal Z_I\mathcal P_S\subset \mathcal Z_I.
\tag{17}
\]

Also, for \(A,B\in\mathcal C\) and \(T\in B(H)\),
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U\in \mathcal Z_I.
\tag{18}
\]
Indeed, by (15),
\[
e_UAe_W
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_U e_J\mathcal C_W:J\subset I\},
\]
and similarly
\[
e_WBe_U
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_W e_K\mathcal C_U:K\subset I\}.
\]

Combining (17) and (18), we get
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U
\subset \mathcal Z_I
\subset X_I.
\tag{19}
\]
Passing to norm limits gives
\[
h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\in X_I
\tag{20}
\]
for all algebraic approximate-unit elements \(h^U_\lambda,h^W_\mu\).

### 5.5. Passing to support projections

Let
\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda),
\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^W_\mu).
\]
Then
\[
a_\lambda\to q_U,
\qquad
b_\mu\to q_W
\]
weak-* in \(\mathcal N\).

Because \(h^U_\lambda,h^W_\mu\in C^*(M,\rho(M),e_U,e_W)\), multiplicativity (14) gives
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda).
\]
By (20), the operator on the right is a positive contraction supported by \(q_I\). Hence
\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{21}
\]
Letting \(\mu\to\infty\) and using separate weak-* continuity of multiplication in \(\mathcal N\),
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{22}
\]
Since \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\) and \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) weak-*, we have \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) strongly. Therefore
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
weak-*. The order interval \([0,q_I]\) is weak-* closed, so (22) yields
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
This proves (13).

---

## 6. The empty parabolic boundary is the compact boundary

For \(U=\varnothing\), we have \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\),
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
=
\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}.
\]
The vectors \(\mathcal A\widehat 1\) are dense in \(L^2(M)\), so the span of these rank-one operators is norm-dense in \(K(H)\). Conversely, the hereditary algebra generated by a rank-one projection is contained in \(K(H)\). Hence
\[
X_\varnothing=K(H),
\]
and therefore
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{23}
\]

---

## 7. Final contradiction

Assume \(M\) is not properly proximal. By Section 4, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. Therefore DKEP Proposition 8.2 gives an obstruction state \(\Omega\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{24}
\]

For every \(v\in V\), Section 3 shows that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Hence, by (3),
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad (v\in V).
\tag{25}
\]

Extend \(\Omega\) to a state \(\overline{\Omega}\) on the \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the normal-bidual boundary operator system and all support projections \(q_U\). The values in (24) and (25) are preserved.

We use the elementary fact that if \(\phi\) is a state and \(p,s\) are projections with
\[
\phi(p)=\phi(s)=1,
\]
then
\[
\phi(psp)=1.
\]
Indeed, in the GNS representation, both \(p\) and \(s\) fix the cyclic vector.

Now suppose
\[
\overline{\Omega}(q_U)=\overline{\Omega}(q_W)=1.
\]
Then
\[
\overline{\Omega}(q_Uq_Wq_U)=1.
\]
By the support sandwich (13),
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
so
\[
\overline{\Omega}(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\tag{26}
\]

Iterating (26) over the finite family
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V,
\]
and using (25), we obtain
\[
\overline{\Omega}\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Because \(\Gamma\) is loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Hence
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Thus
\[
\overline{\Omega}(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]
Using (23), this gives
\[
\overline{\Omega}(q_K)=1,
\]
contradicting (24).

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal. \(\square\)

**Claim proved.**  
Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\), irreducible in the sense that \(\Gamma\) has no nontrivial join decomposition
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\ne\varnothing,
\]
with every vertex of \(V_1\) adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). Let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras, each containing a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\), and let
\[
(M,\tau)=\ast_\Gamma (M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be their tracial graph product. Then:

1. For every \(v\in V\), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to the DKEP boundary piece \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) associated with \(M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\subset M\).

2. \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### 1. Graph-product and DKEP notation

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and let \(e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)\) be the Jones projection.

For \(v\in V\), write
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}: w\sim v\},\qquad
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}.
\]

We use the standard reduced-word facts for tracial graph products: reduced graph words span a weakly dense \(*\)-subalgebra of \(M\), and \(E_U\) kills every reduced word containing a vertex outside \(U\).

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad B=M_L,\qquad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Then
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B,
\]
and
\[
M=A\ast_B C
\]
as a tracial amalgamated free product.

Let
\[
A^\circ=\ker(E_B|_A),\qquad C^\circ=\ker(E_B|_C).
\]
The AFP \(L^2\)-decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where each \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\), consecutive letters are distinct, and
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]

Let \(P_A\) be the projection onto the sum of reduced AFP summands whose first letter is \(A\), and \(P_C\) the projection onto those whose first letter is \(C\). Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]
Define
\[
R_A=e_B+P_A,\qquad R_C=e_B+P_C.
\]

Let \(H=L^2(M)\), and let \(\rho(M)=JMJ\) be the right action. For an expected subalgebra \(P\subset M\), let \(X_P\subset B(H)\) denote the DKEP boundary piece generated by the \(M\)- and \(\rho(M)\)-translates of \(e_PB(H)e_P\). Write \(S_{X_P}(M)\) for the relative DKEP operator system:
\[
S_{X_P}(M)=\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho(x)]\in K^{\infty,1}_{X_P}(M)\ \text{for all }x\in M\}.
\]
Here \(K^{\infty,1}_{X_P}(M)\) is the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed \(M\)-\(\rho(M)\)-bimodule generated by \(X_P\).

By DKEP Theorem 6.2 and the definition following it, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_P\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_P}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.

Thus, for fixed \(v\), it suffices to show that \(S_{X_L}(M)\) has no \(M\)-central state at all.

---

### 2. The first-letter projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let \(X=X_L\).

Right multiplication by elements of \(A\) preserves \(R_AH\): multiplying an AFP reduced word on the right can only affect its final letter, never its initial letter, except that a one-letter word may collapse into \(L^2(B)\), which is still contained in \(R_AH\). Since the same holds for adjoints, \(R_AH\) reduces \(\rho(A)\). Hence
\[
[R_A,\rho(a)]=0\qquad(a\in A).
\]
Similarly,
\[
[R_C,\rho(c)]=0\qquad(c\in C).
\]

Because
\[
R_A+R_C=1+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[R_C,\rho(a)]=[e_B,\rho(a)]\qquad(a\in A),
\]
and
\[
[R_A,\rho(c)]=[e_B,\rho(c)]\qquad(c\in C).
\]
Since \(e_B\in X\) and \(K^{\infty,1}_X(M)\) is an \(M\)-\(\rho(M)\)-bimodule, all commutators \([e_B,\rho(x)]\) lie in \(K^{\infty,1}_X(M)\). Therefore the commutator condition holds for \(R_A\) and \(R_C\) on the algebraic \(*\)-algebra generated by \(A\cup C\).

Now let \(x\in M\). By Kaplansky density and the graph-product normal form, choose algebraic \(x_n\) with \(\sup_n\|x_n\|_\infty<\infty\) and \(\|x_n-x\|_2\to0\). For any bounded \(T\in B(H)\), the standard DKEP estimate gives
\[
\|[T,\rho(x_n-x)]\|_{\infty,1}
\le 2\|T\|\,\|x_n-x\|_2.
\]
Thus \([R_A,\rho(x_n)]\to [R_A,\rho(x)]\) and \([R_C,\rho(x_n)]\to [R_C,\rho(x)]\) in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\). Since \(K^{\infty,1}_X(M)\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed,
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_X(M).
\]
Also \(e_B\in S_X(M)\), hence
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_X(M)\).

---

### 3. Construction of ping-pong unitaries

Because
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\) and set
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is a unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=0,
\]
so \(a\in A^\circ\).

Next, we construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) such that
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of non-neighbors of \(v\). Irreducibility implies \(N(v)\ne\varnothing\); otherwise \(\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})\) would be a nontrivial join decomposition.

If \(|N(v)|\ge2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\subset C.
\]
Since \(w_i\notin L\), each \(c_i\) is \(B\)-centered. Also, for \(i\ne j\), \(c_i^*c_j\) is a reduced graph word containing vertices outside \(L\), so
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0.
\]

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(L\ne\varnothing\) because \(|V|\ge3\). If \(w\) were adjacent to every vertex of \(L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition. Hence there exists \(z\in L\) with \(z\not\sim w\). Choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\), and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries, each has \(B\)-expectation zero, and
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w,\qquad c_2^*c_1=u_w^*u_z^*u_w
\]
are reduced graph words containing the vertex \(w\notin L\). Therefore
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

---

### 4. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_AH,\qquad H_C^+=R_CH.
\]

If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\). Indeed, \(dL^2(B)\subset K_C\), and if a reduced AFP word starts with an \(A\)-letter, prepending a centered \(C\)-letter produces a reduced word starting with \(C\).

Thus, for each \(i\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

Moreover, since \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), we have \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\). Therefore, for \(\xi,\eta\in R_AH\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,c_1^*c_2\eta\rangle
=
0,
\]
because \(c_1^*c_2\eta\in P_CH\perp R_AH\). Hence the ranges \(c_1R_AH\) and \(c_2R_AH\) are orthogonal, and so
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_AH\). Hence
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

---

### 5. Absence of \(M\)-central states on \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Suppose \(\omega\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\). Since \(S_X(M)\) is an \(M\)-bimodule, centrality implies
\[
\omega(uTu^*)=\omega(T)
\]
for every unitary \(u\in M\) and every \(T\in S_X(M)\).

Set
\[
\alpha=\omega(e_B),\qquad \beta=\omega(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\omega(P_C).
\]
Because \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge0,\qquad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Applying \(\omega\) to
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C
\]
gives
\[
2\omega(R_A)\le \omega(P_C),
\]
that is,
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma.
\]

Applying \(\omega\) to
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A
\]
gives
\[
\omega(R_C)\le \omega(P_A),
\]
that is,
\[
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]

Combining the two inequalities,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Thus
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Since \(\alpha,\beta\ge0\), we get \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) gives \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore \(S_{X_L}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. By DKEP Theorem 6.2, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_L=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary, this holds for every vertex \(v\).

---

### 6. No amenable direct summand

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(M\) has an amenable direct summand. Then there is a nonzero central projection \(z\in Z(M)\) such that \(zM\) is amenable.

Because \(zM\) is finite amenable, there exists a \(zM\)-central hypertrace
\[
\psi:B(zL^2(M))\to\mathbb C
\]
extending the normalized trace on \(zM\).

Fix any \(v\in V\), and put again \(X=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). For \(T\in S_X(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi\bigl(zTz|_{zL^2(M)}\bigr).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_X(M)\).

For \(x\in M\) and \(T\in S_X(M)\), since \(z\) is central,
\[
z(xT)z=(zx)(zTz),
\]
and
\[
z(Tx)z=(zTz)(zx).
\]
Because \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central,
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\psi((zx)(zTz))
=
\psi((zTz)(zx))
=
\Phi(Tx).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\), contradicting the result above.

Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. \(\square\)

## Original Problem

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

## Problem Solved

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simple loopless graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). For each \(v\in V\), let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be a separable von Neumann algebra equipped with a faithful normal tracial state, and assume that there is a unitary \(u_v\in M_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\star_\Gamma (M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra, and for \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) denote the graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Assume that \(\Gamma\) is irreducible in the standard intended sense: there is no partition \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) into two nonempty sets such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). Then \(M\) is properly proximal in the sense of Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Peterson: if \(H=L^2(M,\tau)\), \(J\) is the Tomita conjugation, \(X=\mathbb K(H)\), \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\) is the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed compact ideal associated with \(X\), and
\[
S(M)=\{T\in B(H):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \text{for every }x\in M\},
\]
then there is no \(M\)-central state \(\phi:S(M)\to\mathbb C\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. This is the original statement under the standard nontrivial-join interpretation of irreducibility; no weakening is made.

## Proof

### 1. Definitions and external facts

We use the following standard graph-product notation. For \(U\subset V\), \(M_U\) is the von Neumann algebra generated by the vertex algebras \(M_u\), \(u\in U\). There is a trace-preserving conditional expectation
\[
E_U:M\to M_U,
\]
and the algebraic span of graph-reduced Wick words is \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense in \(L^2(M)\). If a reduced word contains a vertex outside \(U\), then its \(E_U\)-expectation is \(0\). These are standard consequences of the Fock-space normal form for graph products and the conditional-expectation formula for relatively reduced words. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

For \(v\in V\), write
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}:\{v,w\}\in E\},\qquad
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}.
\]

We recall the DKEP operator-system formulation. Let \((N,\tau)\) be a tracial von Neumann algebra, \(H_N=L^2(N)\), and \(J_N\) its Tomita conjugation. If \(X\subset B(H_N)\) is an \(N\)-boundary piece, define
\[
K_X^L(N)=\overline{B(H_N)X}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}},
\qquad
K_X(N)=(K_X^L(N))^*\cap K_X^L(N),
\]
and
\[
K_X^{\infty,1}(N)=\overline{K_X(N)}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}.
\]
Here
\[
\|T\|_{\infty,2}=\sup_{\|x\|_\infty\le 1}\|T\widehat x\|_2,\qquad
\|T\|_{\infty,1}=\sup_{\|x\|_\infty,\|y\|_\infty\le 1}
|\langle T\widehat x,\widehat y\rangle|.
\]
Then
\[
S_X(N)=\{T\in B(H_N):[T,J_NxJ_N]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\ \forall x\in N\}.
\]
DKEP define \(N\) to be properly proximal relative to \(X\) if there is no \(N\)-central state on \(S_X(N)\) whose restriction to \(N\) is normal. For \(X=\mathbb K(H_N)\), this is ordinary proper proximality. Boundary pieces associated to subalgebras \(B\subset N\) are generated by the Jones projection \(e_B:L^2(N)\to L^2(B)\): \(X_B\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(xJyJe_B\), \(x,y\in N\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

We also use the normal-bidual package of Ding–Evington/DKEP. For each boundary piece \(X\), let \(q_X\) be the identity/support projection of the normal-bidual compact corner \((K_X(N)^\sharp_J)^*\) inside the ambient normal bidual \((B(L^2N)^\sharp_J)^*\). For \(U\subset V\), write
\[
q_U:=q_{X_U},\qquad X_U:=X_{M_U},
\]
and define
\[
q_K:=q_{\mathbb K(L^2M)}
\]
using the same support construction. We shall use these standard facts:

1. If \(N\) is separable tracial, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there is an \(N\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual operator system, normal on \(N\), such that
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0.
   \]
2. If \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\), then every such obstruction state satisfies
   \[
   \Omega(q_X)=1.
   \]
   Indeed, if \(r=1-q_X\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), the compression
   \[
   T\mapsto \frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}
   \]
   gives an \(N\)-central state on the relative normal-bidual system, normal on \(N\), contradicting relative proper proximality.
3. The supports \(q_X\) commute with the left and right \(N\)-actions, and the compression inclusion
   \[
   (1-q_X)\,\widetilde S_X(N)\,(1-q_X)\subset (1-q_K)\,\widetilde S(N)
   \]
   is valid.
4. If \((h_i)\) is a suitable approximate unit for a \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense compact core of \(X\), then \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h_i)\to q_X\) weak-* in the normal bidual; moreover the normal-bidual compression is multiplicative on \(C^*(N,JNJ,e_B)\) because the normal support projection commutes with \(N\), \(JNJ\), and Jones projections. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

We emphasize that the obstruction state \(\Omega\) is only assumed normal on \(M\), not on the whole ambient normal bidual.

---

### 2. Graph preliminaries

The graph is simple and loopless. Thus, if one tried to use overlapping join factors \(V_1,V_2\) with all cross edges present and \(x\in V_1\cap V_2\), one would require a loop \(\{x,x\}\), impossible. Empty join factors are also excluded by the standard convention. Hence irreducible means: no nontrivial join partition \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) with \(V_1,V_2\ne\varnothing\).

First, for every \(v\in V\),
\[
\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V.
\]
Indeed, if \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(V\setminus\{v\}\), and since \(|V|\ge 3\), the partition
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join partition.

Second, suppose \(v\) has a unique non-neighbor \(w\), i.e.
\[
V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)=\{w\}.
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=V\setminus\{v,w\}
\]
is nonempty because \(|V|\ge 3\). We claim that there exists
\[
z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)
\]
such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\). If every \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) were adjacent to \(w\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \operatorname{Link}(v)
\]
would be a nontrivial join partition: \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\) by definition, and \(w\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\) by assumption. This contradicts irreducibility.

---

### 3. The link-relative AFP ping-pong lemma

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad B=M_L,\qquad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
The graph-product normal form gives a trace-preserving amalgamated free product decomposition
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]
Moreover,
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B,
\]
because \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(L\).

Let \(e_B:L^2(M)\to L^2(B)\) be the Jones projection. In the AFP \(L^2\)-decomposition, write
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
Then
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n,\ \epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus the reduced summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\), and define \(R_C\) analogously. Set
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Thus
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

#### 3.1. The sector projections lie in \(S_{X_B}(M)\)

Let \(\rho(x)=Jx^*J\), so \(\rho(x)\widehat y=\widehat{yx}\). We show that
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and write \(\bar D\) for the other algebra. If \(x\in D\) with \(E_B(x)=0\), then right multiplication by \(x\) preserves the first \(D\)-sector. Hence
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
For the other sector, the only possible change occurs through the \(L^2(B)\)-part, so
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=e_B\rho(x)-\rho(x)e_B.
\]
Both terms lie in the boundary piece \(X_B\): indeed \(\rho(x)e_B=Jx^*Je_B\) is one of the left-right translates generating \(X_B\), and \(e_B\rho(x)\) is its adjoint. Hence
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]\in K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M).
\]

If \(x\in D\) is arbitrary, decompose \(x=E_B(x)+(x-E_B(x))\). Right multiplication by \(E_B(x)\in B\) preserves all AFP first-letter sectors, so the same conclusion holds.

Since \(M\) is generated by \(A\) and \(C\), it follows that \([R_A,\rho(x)]\) and \([R_C,\rho(x)]\) lie in \(K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M)\) for all \(x\in M\). Indeed, the set of such \(x\)’s is a unital \(*\)-subalgebra, because \(K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M)\) is stable under left and right multiplication by \(JMJ\), and it is \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-closed on bounded sets. Thus \(R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M)\). Since \(e_B\in X_B\subset S_{X_B}(M)\), also
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_B}(M)\).

#### 3.2. Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\). Since \(A=M_v\bar\otimes B\), the unitary
\[
a:=u_v\in A
\]
satisfies
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1=0.
\]

We now choose unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) such that
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Because \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V\), \(v\) has at least one non-neighbor.

If \(v\) has two distinct non-neighbors \(w_1,w_2\), choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\) and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and for \(i\ne j\), the reduced word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains vertices outside \(L\), so
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0.
\]
Clearly \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\).

If \(v\) has a unique non-neighbor \(w\), choose, by the graph lemma, a vertex
\[
z\in L
\]
not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\), \(u_z\in M_z\), and set
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\), and
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Also \(c_i^*c_i=1\). For the cross term,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
The graph word \(w,z,w\) is reduced because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), so the two \(w\)-letters cannot be shuffled together and cancelled. This remains true even if \(u_w\) has order two. Since the reduced word contains \(w\notin L\), its \(B\)-expectation is zero:
\[
E_B(u_w^*u_z u_w)=0.
\]
Similarly \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\).

#### 3.3. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+:=R_A L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus P_A L^2(M),
\qquad
H_C^+:=R_C L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus P_C L^2(M).
\]

If \(d\in C\) is a unitary with \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\). Indeed, \(dL^2(B)\subset L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B)\), and if a reduced AFP word begins with an \(A\)-letter, then multiplying on the left by \(d\in C\ominus B\) gives a reduced word beginning with a \(C\)-letter. Therefore
\[
dR_A d^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying this to \(d=c_1,c_2\), we get \(c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C\).

The ranges are orthogonal. Since
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0,
\]
the same argument gives
\[
(c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M),
\]
which is orthogonal to \(H_A^+\). Hence
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*\perp c_2R_Ac_2^*,
\]
and consequently
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C. \tag{3.1}
\]

Similarly, \(a\in A\) satisfies \(E_B(a)=0\), so left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_A L^2(M)\). Thus
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A. \tag{3.2}
\]

#### 3.4. Contradiction for central states

Suppose \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries,
\[
\phi(uTu^*)=\phi(T)
\]
for \(u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}\) and \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\).

Applying \(\phi\) to (3.1),
\[
2\phi(R_A)\le \phi(P_C).
\]
Applying \(\phi\) to (3.2),
\[
\phi(R_C)\le \phi(P_A).
\]
Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Then \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0\) and
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad \phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Therefore
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Thus \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) gives \(\gamma\le0\), hence \(\gamma=0\), contradicting \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1\).

Hence there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). In particular, there is no such state whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\[
X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}=X_B.
\]
Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every vertex \(v\).

---

### 4. No amenable direct summand

We next show that \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) is a central projection such that \(zM\) is amenable. By Connes’ hypertrace characterization of amenability, there exists a \(zM\)-central state
\[
\Psi:B(zL^2M)\to\mathbb C
\]
extending the normalized trace on \(zM\):
\[
\Psi(x)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)},\qquad x\in zM.
\]

Fix any vertex \(v\), and put \(X=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). For \(T\in S_X(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\Psi(zTz|_{zL^2M}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_X(M)\). If \(x\in M\), then \(zx\in zM\), so
\[
\Phi(xT)=\Psi((zx)(zTz))
=\Psi((zTz)(zx))
=\Phi(Tx).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. Its restriction to \(M\) is
\[
\Phi(x)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)},
\]
which is normal. This contradicts the link-relative proper proximality proved above. Therefore \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### 5. Parabolic support sandwich

We prove the key support estimate.

#### Lemma 5.1

For all \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\]

#### Proof

Let \(H=L^2(M)\). For \(a,b\in M\), write
\[
L_aR_b(\widehat x)=\widehat{axb}.
\]
Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic \(*\)-algebra spanned by reduced graph-product words, and set
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
For \(S\subset V\), write \(\mathcal A_S=\mathcal A\cap M_S\) and
\[
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A_S\}.
\]

For \(S\subset V\), define
\[
\mathscr L_S=\{L_aR_b e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\]
where \(e_S\) is the Jones projection onto \(L^2(M_S)\). Let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(\mathscr L_S\):
\[
D_S=\overline{\operatorname{span}}\{sTt^*:s,t\in\mathscr L_S,\ T\in B(H)\}}^{\|\cdot\|}.
\]
Then \(D_S\subset X_S\).

The algebraic core \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Indeed, algebraic words are \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense in \(M\), and if \(x,y\in M\) are approximated in \(\|\cdot\|_2\) by algebraic \(a,b\), then
\[
\|L_xR_y e_S-L_aR_b e_S\|_{\infty,2}
\le
\|y\|_\infty\|x-a\|_2+\|a\|_\infty\|y-b\|_2.
\]
Using
\[
\|ATB\|_{\infty,1}\le \|A^*\|_{\infty,2}\,\|T\|\,\|B\|_{\infty,2},
\]
one obtains \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-density for the hereditary span.

For a finite set \(F\subset\mathscr L_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), put
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
The net \((h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\) is a positive contraction approximate unit for \(D_S\). Also
\[
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\in C^*(M,JMJ,e_S).
\]
By the DE approximate-unit support lemma,
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\xrightarrow[\ F,\varepsilon\ ]{\ \mathrm{weak}^*\ } q_S. \tag{5.1}
\]

We now prove the finite-propagation estimate:
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}. \tag{5.2}
\]
It suffices to consider \(L_aR_b\) with \(a,b\in\mathcal A\) reduced Wick words.

Using the relative graph-product normal form, decompose \(a\) relative to \((S,T)\) as
\[
a=a_Sa_0a_T,
\]
where \(a_S\in\mathcal A_S\), \(a_T\in\mathcal A_T\), and \(a_0\) is reduced relative to \((S,T)\). Similarly decompose \(b\) relative to \((T,S)\) as
\[
b=b_Tb_0b_S,
\]
where \(b_T\in\mathcal A_T\), \(b_S\in\mathcal A_S\), and \(b_0\) is reduced relative to \((T,S)\). Then \(a_0^*\) is also reduced relative to \((T,S)\).

For \(z\in\mathcal A_T\),
\[
e_SL_aR_b e_T\widehat z
=
\widehat{E_S(azb)}
=
\widehat{a_S\,E_S(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0)\,b_S}.
\]
Apply the graph-product conditional expectation formula with \(V_1=T\), \(V_2=S\), \(y=a_Tzb_T\), \(\widetilde x=a_0^*\), and \(x=b_0\). If the middle words are not equivalent, the expectation is \(0\). If they are equivalent, then
\[
E_S(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0)
=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,E_J(a_Tzb_T),
\]
where \(J\subset S\cap T\) is the set of vertices in \(S\cap T\) adjacent to all middle letters. Hence
\[
e_SL_aR_b e_T
=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,
L_{a_S}R_{b_S}\,e_J\,L_{a_T}R_{b_T}e_T.
\]
This proves (5.2).

Now define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}\{C_0e_SC_1e_S\cdots e_SC_n:
n\ge1,\ C_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) lies in the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\): \(A_{F,S}\in\mathcal P_S\), \(\mathcal P_S\) is an algebra, and the function \(t\mapsto t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is uniformly approximable on spectra by polynomials vanishing at \(0\).

Let
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}\{A e_J T e_K B:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\), we have \(e_JTe_K\in e_I B(H)e_I\), and therefore
\[
\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I. \tag{5.3}
\]

We record two consequences of (5.2). First, if \(S=U\) or \(S=W\), then
\[
\mathcal P_S\mathcal Z_I\subset\mathcal Z_I,\qquad
\mathcal Z_I\mathcal P_S\subset\mathcal Z_I. \tag{5.4}
\]
Indeed, \(I\subset S\). If \(J\subset I\) and \(C\in\mathcal C\), then (5.2) gives
\[
e_SC e_J\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_L\mathcal C_J:L\subset J\},
\]
and similarly for \(e_JCe_S\). Thus multiplying by one \(e_S\)-block keeps all intermediate Jones projections supported inside \(I\), and iteration proves (5.4).

Second, for \(A,B\in\mathcal C\) and \(T\in B(H)\),
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U\in\mathcal Z_I. \tag{5.5}
\]
Indeed, by (5.2),
\[
e_UAe_W=\sum_r A_r e_{J_r}B_r,\qquad
e_WBe_U=\sum_s C_s e_{K_s}D_s,
\]
with \(J_r,K_s\subset I\). Hence
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U
=
\sum_{r,s}A_r e_{J_r}(B_rTC_s)e_{K_s}D_s\in\mathcal Z_I.
\]

Now take monomials \(P\in\mathcal P_U\), \(Q\in\mathcal P_W\), and \(R\in\mathcal P_U\). Inside the product \(PQR\), isolate the bridge from the last \(e_U\)-block of \(P\) to the first \(e_W\)-block of \(Q\), and from the last \(e_W\)-block of \(Q\) to the first \(e_U\)-block of \(R\). The middle portion has the form covered by (5.5), hence lies in \(\mathcal Z_I\). The remaining prefix and suffix preserve \(\mathcal Z_I\) by (5.4). Thus
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U\subset\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I. \tag{5.6}
\]
Passing to norm closures gives
\[
h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\in X_I\subset K_{X_I}(M) \tag{5.7}
\]
for the approximate units above.

Set
\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda),\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^W_\mu).
\]
By (5.1),
\[
a_\lambda\to q_U,\qquad b_\mu\to q_W
\]
weak-*.

The normal-bidual map \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is not multiplicative on all of \(B(H)\). However, it is multiplicative on
\[
C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W),
\]
because the normal support projection commutes with the universal images of \(M\), \(JMJ\), \(e_U\), and \(e_W\). Therefore
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda).
\]
By (5.7), the right-hand side is a positive contraction in the \(q_I\)-corner. Hence
\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I. \tag{5.8}
\]

Fix \(\lambda\) and let \(\mu\) tend to infinity. Separate weak-* continuity of multiplication in the ambient von Neumann algebra gives
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I. \tag{5.9}
\]
Finally, let \(\lambda\) tend to infinity. Since \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\) and \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) weak-*, we have \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) strongly: for every normal positive functional \(\omega\),
\[
\omega((q_U-a_\lambda)^2)\le \omega(q_U-a_\lambda)\to0.
\]
Therefore
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
weak-*. The order interval \([0,q_I]\) is weak-* closed, so (5.9) implies
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\]

This proves the support sandwich. Notice that we did not prove or use commutativity of \(q_U\) and \(q_W\), did not claim \(q_Uq_W=q_{U\cap W}\), and did not claim \(X_UX_W\subset X_{U\cap W}\).

---

### 6. The empty parabolic piece

We prove
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]

Since \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), the Jones projection \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). Its left-right \(M\)-translates generate finite-rank operators. For example,
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
\]
is the rank-one operator \(\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}\) up to the usual trace pairing, and vectors of the form \(\widehat a\), \(a\in M\), are dense in \(L^2(M)\). Hence the hereditary boundary piece generated by \(e_\varnothing\) is exactly
\[
X_\varnothing=\mathbb K(L^2M).
\]
Because \(q_\varnothing\) and \(q_K\) are defined by the same normal-bidual support construction, it follows that
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]

---

### 7. Final obstruction argument

The graph product \(M\) is separable because \(V\) is finite and the algebraic graph-product span built from countable dense subsets of the \(M_v\)’s is \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense in \(L^2(M)\).

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. By Section 4, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. Therefore the DKEP/DE obstruction theorem gives an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual operator system, normal on \(M\), with
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0. \tag{7.1}
\]

For every vertex \(v\in V\), Section 3 showed that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Hence the normal-bidual compression argument gives
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V. \tag{7.2}
\]

Extend \(\Omega\), if necessary, to a state \(\overline\Omega\) on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the relevant support projections \(q_U\). The values in (7.1) and (7.2) are preserved.

We use the elementary support-one fact: if \(\psi\) is a state on a \(C^*\)-algebra and \(p\) is a projection with \(\psi(p)=1\), then in the GNS representation \((\pi_\psi,H_\psi,\xi_\psi)\),
\[
\pi_\psi(p)\xi_\psi=\xi_\psi.
\]
Consequently, if \(\psi(p)=\psi(r)=1\), then
\[
\psi(prp)=1.
\]

Now suppose \(\overline\Omega(q_U)=\overline\Omega(q_W)=1\). Then
\[
\overline\Omega(q_Uq_Wq_U)=1.
\]
By the support sandwich,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
so
\[
\overline\Omega(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]

Iterating this over the finite family
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V,
\]
and using (7.2), we obtain
\[
\overline\Omega\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Because the graph is loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Hence
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]
By Section 6, \(q_\varnothing=q_K\). Thus
\[
1=\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=\overline\Omega(q_K)=\Omega(q_K)=0,
\]
a contradiction.

Hence \(M\) is properly proximal. ∎

## Claim

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simple loopless graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). Interpret irreducibility in the standard nontrivial-join sense: there is no partition \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) into two nonempty sets such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). For each \(v\in V\), let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra containing a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Then the tracial graph product
\[
(M,\tau)=\star_\Gamma (M_v,\tau_v)
\]
is properly proximal.

We use the Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Peterson definition of proper proximality for finite von Neumann algebras and the normal-bidual support formalism of Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.00517))

---

## Proof

For \(U\subset V\), write \(M_U\subset M\) for the graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and write \(E_U:M\to M_U\) for the trace-preserving conditional expectation. Let \(H=L^2(M,\tau)\), let \(J\) be the Tomita conjugation, and let \(e_U:H\to L^2(M_U)\) be the Jones projection. We use the usual graph-product reduced-word normal form: \(E_U\) kills every reduced Wick word containing a vertex outside \(U\). We also use the standard relative reduced-word decomposition for graph products. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/journals/jncg/articles/14680))

For a boundary piece \(X\subset B(H)\), define
\[
K_X^L(M)=\overline{B(H)X}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}},\qquad
K_X(M)=K_X^L(M)\cap K_X^L(M)^*,
\]
and
\[
K_X^{\infty,1}(M)=\overline{K_X(M)}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}},
\]
where
\[
\|T\|_{\infty,2}=\sup_{\|x\|_\infty\le 1}\|T\widehat x\|_2,
\]
and
\[
\|T\|_{\infty,1}
=\sup_{\|x\|_\infty,\|y\|_\infty\le 1}
|\langle T\widehat x,\widehat y\rangle|.
\]
Set
\[
S_X(M)=\{T\in B(H):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\}.
\]
Proper proximality relative to \(X\) means that there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. Ordinary proper proximality is the case \(X=\mathbb K(H)\).

For \(U\subset V\), let \(X_U\) denote the boundary piece associated with \(M_U\), i.e. the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra of \(B(H)\) generated by the left-right translates of \(e_U\). Let \(q_U=q_{X_U}\) be the corresponding normal-bidual support projection, and let \(q_K\) be the support projection for \(X=\mathbb K(H)\).

We shall use the following standard bidual package. If \(N\) is separable tracial and \(X\) is an \(N\)-boundary piece, then:

1. \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(N\)-central state on the normal-bidual operator system \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) normal on \(N\).
2. If \(N\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, then there is an \(N\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\), normal on \(N\), such that \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).
3. \(q_X\) commutes with the left and right \(N\)-actions, and
   \[
   (1-q_X)\widetilde S_X(N)(1-q_X)\subset (1-q_K)\widetilde S(N).
   \]
4. If \(D\subset K_X(N)\) is a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra whose \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure is \(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), and \((h_i)\) is a positive contraction approximate unit for \(D\), then \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h_i)\to q_X\) weak-* in the normal bidual. Moreover, if the normal support projection \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with a \(C^*\)-algebra \(\mathcal C\subset B(L^2N)\), then \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is multiplicative on \(\mathcal C\).

A useful consequence is the following: if \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\), then every obstruction state \(\Omega\) as in (2) satisfies \(\Omega(q_X)=1\). Indeed, if \(r=1-q_X\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), then
\[
\psi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)},\qquad T\in \widetilde S_X(N),
\]
is well-defined by (3), is \(N\)-central because \(q_X\) commutes with \(N\), and is normal on \(N\). For \(x\ge 0\) in \(N\),
\[
0\le r x r\le x,
\]
so the restriction of \(x\mapsto \Omega(rxr)\) to \(N\) is dominated by the normal functional \(\Omega|_N\), hence is normal. Thus \(\psi\) contradicts relative proper proximality. Therefore \(\Omega(1-q_X)=0\), i.e. \(\Omega(q_X)=1\).

### 1. Two graph facts

For \(v\in V\), write
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}:\{v,w\}\in E\},
\qquad
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}.
\]

First, \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V\) for every \(v\). Otherwise
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join partition.

Second, suppose \(v\) has a unique non-neighbor \(w\), i.e.
\[
V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)=\{w\}.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{Link}(v)=V\setminus\{v,w\}\) is nonempty because \(|V|\ge 3\). There exists \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) not adjacent to \(w\). Indeed, if every \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) were adjacent to \(w\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \operatorname{Link}(v)
\]
would be a nontrivial join partition, contradiction.

### 2. Relative proper proximality over links

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad
B=M_L,\qquad
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
The graph-product normal form gives
\[
M=A*_B C,
\]
and because \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B.
\]

Let \(X=X_L\) be the boundary piece associated with \(B=M_L\). In the AFP decomposition, set
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
Then
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n,\ \epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus the reduced summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\), and define \(R_C\) similarly. Let
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

We claim that
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_X(M).
\]
Let \(\rho(x)=Jx^*J\), so \(\rho(x)\widehat y=\widehat{yx}\). Fix \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\overline D\) be the other algebra. If \(x\in D\) satisfies \(E_B(x)=0\), then right multiplication by \(x\) preserves the first-\(D\) sector, so
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
Since \(R_{\overline D}=e_B+1-R_D\), we have
\[
[R_{\overline D},\rho(x)]
=e_B\rho(x)-\rho(x)e_B.
\]
Both \(e_B\rho(x)\) and \(\rho(x)e_B\) belong to \(X\subset K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\). If \(x\in D\) is arbitrary, write \(x=E_B(x)+(x-E_B(x))\); right multiplication by \(E_B(x)\in B\) preserves all first-letter sectors, so the same conclusion holds.

For fixed \(R\in\{R_A,R_C\}\), define
\[
\mathcal N_R=\{x\in M:[R,\rho(x)]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\}.
\]
Because \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\) is a \(JMJ\)-bimodule, \(\mathcal N_R\) is a unital \(*\)-subalgebra of \(M\). Also, if \(x_n\to x\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\) with \(\sup_n\|x_n\|_\infty<\infty\), then
\[
\|[R,\rho(x_n-x)]\|_{\infty,1}
\le 2\|R\|\,\|x_n-x\|_2,
\]
so \(\mathcal N_R\) is closed under bounded \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-limits. Since \(A\cup C\subset \mathcal N_R\) and \(A,C\) generate \(M=A*_B C\), Kaplansky density gives \(\mathcal N_R=M\). Hence \(R_A,R_C\in S_X(M)\), and since \(e_B\in X\subset S_X(M)\), also \(P_A,P_C\in S_X(M)\).

Now choose a trace-zero unitary \(a=u_v\in M_v\subset A\). Since \(A=M_v\bar\otimes B\),
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1=0.
\]

We next choose unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) such that
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Because \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V\), \(v\) has at least one non-neighbor.

If \(v\) has two distinct non-neighbors \(w_1,w_2\), choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\) and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and for \(i\ne j\), the reduced word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains a vertex outside \(L\), so
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0.
\]

If \(v\) has a unique non-neighbor \(w\), choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\), \(u_z\in M_z\), and set
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\), \(E_B(c_1)=0\), and
\[
E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Also \(c_i^*c_i=1\). For the cross term,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
The word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced because \(w\) and \(z\) are not adjacent. It contains the vertex \(w\notin L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Similarly \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\).

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_AH,\qquad H_C^+=R_CH.
\]
If \(d\in C\) is a unitary with \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) maps \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\). Thus
\[
dR_A d^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying this to \(c_1,c_2\), we get \(c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C\). Moreover, since \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), the ranges \(c_1H_A^+\) and \(c_2H_A^+\) are orthogonal. Hence
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C. \tag{2.1}
\]
Similarly, since \(a\in A\) and \(E_B(a)=0\), left multiplication by \(a\) maps \(H_C^+\) into \(P_AH\), so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A. \tag{2.2}
\]

Suppose \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\). Applying \(\phi\) to (2.1) and (2.2), and using \(M\)-centrality, gives
\[
2\phi(R_A)\le \phi(P_C),\qquad
\phi(R_C)\le \phi(P_A).
\]
Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad
\beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad
\gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Then \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0\), \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1\), and
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad
\phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Therefore
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so \(3\alpha+\beta\le 0\). Thus \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le \beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1\).

Hence there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\). Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Since \(v\) was arbitrary,
\[
M\ \text{is properly proximal relative to}\ X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\quad\forall v\in V. \tag{2.3}
\]

### 3. \(M\) has no amenable direct summand

Assume \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) is a central projection such that \(zM\) is amenable. By Connes’ hypertrace characterization, there is a \(zM\)-central state
\[
\Psi:B(zH)\to \mathbb C
\]
extending the normalized trace on \(zM\):
\[
\Psi(x)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)},\qquad x\in zM.
\]

Fix \(v\in V\), and put \(X=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). For \(T\in S_X(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\Psi(zTz|_{zH}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_X(M)\). If \(x\in M\), then \(zx\in zM\), and
\[
\Phi(xT)=\Psi((zx)(zTz))
=\Psi((zTz)(zx))
=\Phi(Tx).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. Its restriction to \(M\) is
\[
\Phi(x)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)},
\]
which is normal. This contradicts (2.3). Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

### 4. The support sandwich

We prove that for all \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I. \tag{4.1}
\]

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic \(*\)-algebra spanned by reduced graph-product Wick words. For \(S\subset V\), let \(\mathcal A_S=\mathcal A\cap M_S\), and define
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\qquad
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A_S\},
\]
where \(L_a\) and \(R_b\) denote left and right multiplication.

We first record a finite-propagation estimate. For \(S,T\subset V\),
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}. \tag{4.2}
\]
Indeed, it suffices to consider \(L_aR_b\) with \(a,b\) reduced Wick words. Using relative normal form, after finite linear expansion we may write
\[
a=a_Sa_0a_T,\qquad b=b_Tb_0b_S,
\]
where
\[
a_S,b_S\in\mathcal A_S,\qquad
a_T,b_T\in\mathcal A_T,
\]
and \(a_0^*,b_0\) are reduced relative to \((T,S)\). For \(z\in\mathcal A_T\),
\[
e_SL_aR_be_T\widehat z
=
\widehat{E_S(a z b)}
=
\widehat{a_S E_S(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0)b_S}.
\]
The relative graph-product conditional-expectation formula gives zero unless \(a_0^*\) and \(b_0\) are equivalent reduced words. In the equivalent case,
\[
E_S(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0)
=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,E_J(a_Tzb_T),
\]
where \(J\subset S\cap T\) is the set of vertices in \(S\cap T\) adjacent to every middle letter. Hence
\[
e_SL_aR_be_T
=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,L_{a_S}R_{b_S}e_JL_{a_T}R_{b_T}.
\]
Because \(J\subset T\), one has
\[
e_JL_{a_T}R_{b_T}=e_JL_{a_T}R_{b_T}e_T,
\]
so the displayed operator lies in \(\mathcal C_Se_J\mathcal C_T\). Linearity proves (4.2).

Now fix \(S\subset V\). Let
\[
\mathscr L_S=\{L_aR_b e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\]
and let \(D_S\) be the norm-closed hereditary \(C^*\)-algebra generated by \(\mathscr L_S\):
\[
D_S=\overline{\operatorname{span}}\{sTt^*:s,t\in\mathscr L_S,\ T\in B(H)\}}^{\|\cdot\|}.
\]
Then \(D_S\subset X_S\subset K_{X_S}(M)\).

We need that \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Let \(x,y\in M\), and choose uniformly bounded algebraic approximants \(a,b\in\mathcal A\). Then
\[
\|L_xR_y e_S-L_aR_b e_S\|_{\infty,2}
\le
\|y\|_\infty\|x-a\|_2+\|a\|_\infty\|y-b\|_2.
\]
The same estimate holds for adjoints. Also, for bounded operators \(A,T,B\),
\[
\|ATB\|_{\infty,1}
\le
\|A^*\|_{\infty,2}\,\|T\|\,\|B\|_{\infty,2}.
\]
Thus hereditary products built from arbitrary \(L_xR_y e_S\) are \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-approximated by hereditary products built from algebraic translates. Therefore \(D_S\) is a \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense compact core for \(X_S\).

For a finite set \(F\subset\mathscr L_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), put
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
The net \((h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\) is a positive contraction approximate unit for \(D_S\). Moreover,
\[
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\in C^*(M,JMJ,e_S).
\]
By the bidual approximate-unit support lemma,
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\longrightarrow q_S
\]
weak-*.

Next define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}
\{C_0e_SC_1e_S\cdots e_SC_n:
n\ge 1,\ C_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) belongs to the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\): indeed \(A_{F,S}\in\mathcal P_S\), \(\mathcal P_S\) is an algebra, and \(t\mapsto t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is uniformly approximable on spectra by polynomials vanishing at \(0\).

Let \(I=U\cap W\), and set
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}\{A e_J T e_K B:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\), we have
\[
\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\]

Using (4.2), if \(S=U\) or \(S=W\), then
\[
\mathcal P_S\mathcal Z_I\subset\mathcal Z_I,
\qquad
\mathcal Z_I\mathcal P_S\subset\mathcal Z_I. \tag{4.3}
\]
Indeed, \(I\subset S\), and (4.2) gives
\[
e_SC e_J\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_Se_L\mathcal C_J:L\subset J\}
\]
whenever \(J\subset I\); similarly for \(e_JCe_S\). Thus multiplying by an \(e_S\)-block preserves the condition that all intermediate Jones projections are supported inside \(I\).

Again by (4.2), for \(A,B\in\mathcal C\) and \(T\in B(H)\),
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U\in\mathcal Z_I. \tag{4.4}
\]
Indeed,
\[
e_UAe_W=\sum_r A_r e_{J_r}B_r,\qquad
e_WBe_U=\sum_s C_s e_{K_s}D_s,
\]
with \(J_r,K_s\subset I\), so the product belongs to \(\mathcal Z_I\).

Now take monomials \(P\in\mathcal P_U\), \(Q\in\mathcal P_W\), \(R\in\mathcal P_U\). In \(PQR\), isolate the bridge from the last \(e_U\)-block before \(Q\) to the first \(e_W\)-block of \(Q\), and from the last \(e_W\)-block of \(Q\) to the first \(e_U\)-block of \(R\). The middle bridge is covered by (4.4), hence lies in \(\mathcal Z_I\); the remaining prefix and suffix preserve \(\mathcal Z_I\) by (4.3). Therefore
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U\subset\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I. \tag{4.5}
\]

Let \(h_\lambda^U\) and \(h_\mu^W\) be the approximate units constructed above. By (4.5),
\[
h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U\in X_I.
\]
Set
\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h_\lambda^U),
\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h_\mu^W).
\]
Then
\[
a_\lambda\to q_U,\qquad b_\mu\to q_W
\]
weak-*. Since \(h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U\in C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\), and \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with every generator of this \(C^*\)-algebra, \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is multiplicative on it:
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(xy)
=p_{\mathrm{nor}}\iota(xy)p_{\mathrm{nor}}
=p_{\mathrm{nor}}\iota(x)p_{\mathrm{nor}}\iota(y)p_{\mathrm{nor}}
=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(x)\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(y).
\]
Hence
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U).
\]
The right-hand side is a positive contraction lying in the \(q_I\)-corner, so
\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Letting \(\mu\) tend to infinity gives
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Finally \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\) and \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) weak-*. Thus \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) strongly, because for every normal positive functional \(\omega\),
\[
\omega((q_U-a_\lambda)^2)\le \omega(q_U-a_\lambda)\to 0.
\]
Therefore
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
weak-*, and the order interval \([0,q_I]\) is weak-* closed. Hence
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\]
This proves the support sandwich (4.1).

### 5. The empty parabolic support is the compact support

Since \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), the Jones projection \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). For \(a,b\in M\),
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
\]
is the rank-one operator \(\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}\). Vectors \(\widehat a\), \(a\in M\), are dense in \(H\), so the hereditary boundary piece generated by \(e_\varnothing\) is exactly \(\mathbb K(H)\). Hence
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K. \tag{5.1}
\]

### 6. Final obstruction argument

The algebra \(M\) has separable predual because \(V\) is finite and the algebraic graph-product span built from countable \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense subsets of the \(M_v\)’s is \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense in \(L^2(M)\).

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. By Section 3, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. Hence the bidual obstruction theorem gives an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0. \tag{6.1}
\]
For every \(v\in V\), Section 2 shows that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Therefore
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V. \tag{6.2}
\]

Extend \(\Omega\) to a state \(\overline\Omega\) on the \(C^*\)-algebra generated by \(\widetilde S(M)\) and all projections \(q_U\), \(U\subset V\). The values in (6.1) and (6.2) are unchanged.

If \(\psi\) is a state and \(p\) is a projection with \(\psi(p)=1\), then in the GNS representation, \(\pi(p)\xi=\xi\). Hence if \(\psi(p)=\psi(r)=1\), then
\[
\psi(prp)=1.
\]
Now suppose \(\overline\Omega(q_U)=\overline\Omega(q_W)=1\). Then
\[
\overline\Omega(q_Uq_Wq_U)=1.
\]
By the support sandwich,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W},
\]
so
\[
\overline\Omega(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]
Iterating over the finite family
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V,
\]
we obtain
\[
\overline\Omega\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Because \(\Gamma\) is loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Therefore
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Thus
\[
\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]
By (5.1), \(q_\varnothing=q_K\), so
\[
1=\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)
=\overline\Omega(q_K)
=\Omega(q_K)
=0,
\]
a contradiction.

Hence \(M\) is properly proximal. ∎

**Claim.** Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simple loopless graph with \(|V|\ge 3\).
